assumptions: Baseline topology: single account on a clustered server with JetStream, streams and durable consumers at R=3; facts marked with conditions (e.g. raft only when R>1) are scored for that clustered case.
Core NATS facts assume no leafnode/gateway amplification; propagation costs appear as scaling factors.
Client RTTs count awaited round trips on the happy path; fire-and-forget operations are 0 even though bytes still flow.
Disk I/O reflects the default JetStream file store; memory-storage streams trade the writes for RAM.
The tier is computed by the documented rubric from these facts; it is relative burden per call, not an absolute throughput claim.

choke_points score shared serialization rather than per-call work: shared-api-pool = the bounded, deprioritized $JS.API worker pool (min(GOMAXPROCS,16) workers; past the 10k queue limit the server drops all pending requests), asset-lock = a data-path write lock held per call.

axes.name: Round trips
axes.measures: The number of client-awaited network round trips on the operation's happy path.
axes.why: A round trip is not free: each awaited RTT is a full network traversal plus a protocol frame the server must parse and route, and the caller blocks on it. For request-reply the server performs two publishes (request out, reply back) through existing interest, so latency-bound callers serialize on the RTT count — a per-message DoubleAck or a paged List multiplies it by the workload. Paged/list APIs are capped at the same 2 points as a multi-RTT handshake because from the rubric's fixed-fact standpoint their round-trip count is unbounded.

levels.label: Fire-and-forget or local-only: no awaited reply or PONG (publish, ack, NextMsg)
One request-reply round trip; the client blocks for a single reply
Two or more awaited round trips (connect INFO/CONNECT+PING handshake, drain)
Paged/unbounded round trips: one request per page (ListStreams, ListConsumers)

axes.name: Disk I/O
axes.measures: Whether serving the call touches the file store for a read or a write.
axes.why: The JetStream file store is block-organized: messages are packed into blocks (msgBlock) sized up to 8MB (defaultLargeBlockSize; small/medium/large are 1/4/8MB) whose bytes live on disk until faulted into the in-memory block cache. A cold read must load an entire block via loadBlock before cacheLookup can serve one message, so a single GetMsg or delivery can drag a whole block off disk, and the cache expires after ~10s idle (defaultCacheBufferExpiration). A write (StoreMsg) appends the record, updates the per-subject index and tombstone accounting, and dirties the block for the batched background fsync (SyncInterval default 2m); write scores higher than read because it additionally indexes and must be persisted/replicated rather than merely faulted.

levels.label: No file-store access (in-memory state read, or memory-storage stream)
Loads a stored message; a cold read faults an entire block (up to 8MB) into the cache
Appends/erases a record, updates the per-subject index, dirties the block for fsync

axes.name: Raft consensus
axes.measures: Whether the call issues a Raft proposal that must reach quorum before it completes.
axes.why: A propose is not a local write. On the leader, Propose/ProposeMulti only push the entry onto an in-memory proposal queue (n.prop); the entry must then replicate to a quorum of the R=3 group, and every replica persists it to its own write-ahead log via storeToWAL (n.wal.StoreMsg) before it commits and applies. A single R>1 JetStream write or ack is therefore a network fan-out plus a disk append on each peer, and the client's PubAck/confirmation is only sent after commit+apply. All three propose subtypes score the same 2 because the consensus round dominates regardless of which raft group (meta, stream, or consumer) carries it.

levels.label: Leader-local or R1: no consensus round (core NATS, limits-retention R1 ack)
Control-plane proposal on the meta group (stream/consumer create, update, delete)
Data-plane proposal on the stream's raft group (persisted publish, purge, delete-msg)
Delivered/ack-state proposal on the consumer's raft group (ack, delivery, InProgress)

axes.name: Server-side state
axes.measures: The durability class of the state the server must allocate and track for the call.
axes.why: This axis counts footprint the server carries beyond the call itself. Ephemeral state — a connection's client struct and its two read/write goroutines, an ephemeral consumer, a sublist entry — lives in memory and is reaped on disconnect or after InactiveThreshold. Persistent state — a stream, a durable consumer — is materialized on every replica as directories, store files, and its own raft group; it survives restarts and must be recovered, snapshotted, and replicated. Persistent scores 2 because it is a standing cluster-wide obligation, not a transient allocation.

levels.label: No lasting state allocated (publish, get, one-shot info)
In-memory state reaped on disconnect/inactivity (connection, subscription, ephemeral consumer)
Durable, replicated, restart-surviving asset (stream, durable consumer)

axes.name: Collection scan
axes.measures: Whether answering the call requires walking a collection rather than reading O(1) state.
axes.why: Most info calls are O(1) reads of in-memory state, but some must enumerate a collection. A bounded scan touches a small, config-capped set — AccountInfo aggregates usage over an account's stream reservations — so its cost is limited. A full scan enumerates every stream in an account or every consumer on a stream (jsStreamListRequest, jsConsumerListRequest), paged at JSApiNamesLimit=1024 names / JSApiListLimit=256 infos per request, and LIST additionally scatter-gathers live state from each asset leader; cost grows with fleet size and every page rides the shared API worker pool.

levels.label: O(1) state read, no enumeration
Walks a small config-bounded set (account usage aggregation)
Enumerates the whole stream/consumer set, paged and scatter-gathered

axes.name: Choke points
axes.measures: Injected serialization the call imposes on shared server resources, summed across the resources it hits.
axes.why: This axis scores contention rather than per-call work. shared-api-pool (1): all non-direct JS API traffic funnels through ONE bounded worker pool per server sized min(GOMAXPROCS,16) in setJetStreamExportSubs, and INFO-class requests ride a second queue that processJSAPIRoutedRequests services only when the main queue is empty (deprioritized via a nested select); past the queue limit apiDispatch calls queue.drain() and drops EVERY pending request, emitting JSAdvisoryAPILimitReached. asset-lock (2): the call takes a data-path write lock on someone else's hot path — CONSUMER.INFO's infoWithSnapAndReply grabs o.mu, the same mutex loopAndGatherMsgs delivers under, so info polls serialize against delivery; subscribe/unsubscribe take the account sublist write lock that every publish matches through; purge holds mset.mu for its whole duration. Points sum, so an op that both rides the pool and grabs an asset lock (purge, consumer info) scores 3.

levels.label: Rides the bounded, deprioritized $JS.API worker pool with drop-all overflow
Takes a data-path write lock on another hot path (delivery mutex, account sublist, stream lock)

axes.name: Steady state (double-weighted)
axes.measures: The recurring per-message and per-interval work a long-lived operation imposes for its whole lifetime, weighted double.
axes.why: Long-lived operations (consumers, watchers, subscriptions) cost for their entire lifetime, not once, so their recurring work is scored separately and then doubled. The steady term sums recurring disk_io (every delivered message is a store read via LoadNextMsg; every persisted publish a write), recurring raft (replicated consumers propose delivered-state per message via ProposeMulti — a quorum round before send), a per-message server hook (loopAndGatherMsgs delivery bookkeeping), and interval ops (idle heartbeats, ack-wait redelivery timers, pull-expiry sweeps). Because this burden recurs rather than being paid once, its contribution to the final score is multiplied by two.

levels.label: Per-message durable append (steady 3, x2 = 6): every persisted delivery/publish writes the store
Per-message store read (steady 2, x2 = 4): each delivery loads from the file store
Per-message consensus round (steady 2, x2 = 4): replicated consumers propose delivered/ack state
Per-message server hook (steady 1, x2 = 2): delivery/match/ack bookkeeping on every message
Recurring timers (steady 1, x2 = 2): heartbeats, redelivery timers, expiry sweeps
No steady_state block: a one-shot operation with no recurring tail

cost_model.steady_weight_rationale: Invocation cost is paid once when the call returns; steady-state cost recurs for the entire lifetime of a consumer, subscription, or watcher — every delivered message and every timer tick. A consumer that does a store read and runs a raft round per message pays that burden thousands of times per second, dwarfing its one-time setup. Doubling the steady term keeps a cheap-to-create-but-forever-expensive operation (Consume, PushConsume) from scoring below a one-shot control-plane call, reflecting that recurring work, not setup, dominates total system load.
cost_model.score_reading: A score is the summed relative burden one invocation (plus its doubled steady-state tail) places on the server and cluster, bucketed into tiers: minimal (0-1), low (2-3), moderate (4-5), high (>=6). It ranks operations against each other by how heavy each call is — a durable replicated publish (write + raft + persistent) sits far above a fire-and-forget core publish — not how many ops/sec the server can sustain; it is relative per-call burden, never an absolute throughput number. Read it as "how heavy is one call, and does it keep costing after it returns," and use the per-axis facts to re-derive the tier when your topology (replica count, storage type, direct-get) differs from the R=3 file-store baseline.

operations.summary: Establish a client connection: TCP (+TLS), INFO/CONNECT exchange, authentication.

steady_state.client_traffic: PING every PingInterval (client default 2m); server enforces its own ping_interval

considerations.title: Reuse connections, don't churn them
considerations.note: Every connect and disconnect allocates and tears down server-side state — a client struct, a subscriptions map, outbound buffers, and two dedicated goroutines — and grabs the server-global mutex several times. Open one long-lived connection per process and multiplex subjects over it; connecting per operation is the classic hidden cost.

considerations.title: TLS and auth multiply the cost
considerations.note: TLS adds handshake round trips and CPU, and authentication cost depends on the mechanism: JWT signature verification and bcrypt for hashed passwords are markedly heavier than nkey or token auth. Because both land on every (re)connect, they compound sharply under connection churn.

considerations.title: Connect fanout on $SYS
considerations.note: When a system account is configured, each connect and disconnect publishes a $SYS.ACCOUNT.*.CONNECT / DISCONNECT event. A fleet that reconnects frequently turns this into steady system-account traffic that every monitoring subscriber then has to consume.

considerations.title: ForceReconnect repeats the full handshake
considerations.note: ForceReconnect tears down the session and repeats the entire TCP/TLS/auth handshake against another (or the same) server, paying the whole invocation cost again. Use it deliberately for rebalancing, not as a routine recovery step.

steps.label: The client dials in {0}; the server accepts the TCP (and, if configured, TLS) connection, allocates per-connection state in {1}, and sends its INFO.
The client answers in {0} with CONNECT (credentials and options) plus a PING to flush the handshake.
The server authenticates the client in {0} and replies PONG; the connection is now live.
When a system account is configured, {0} publishes a $SYS connect event for the new session.
Steady state: keepalive PING/PONG in both directions via {0} (client default every 2m), plus connz/statsz accounting.

operations.summary: Fire-and-forget publish of one message to a subject (PUB/HPUB).

considerations.title: Scales with subscriber fanout and payload size
considerations.note: The server does one parse and one interest lookup, then one write per matching subscriber, so a publish to a heavily-subscribed subject costs far more than a lightly-subscribed one. Message size adds to every one of those writes, so large payloads multiply with the fanout.

considerations.title: Subject stability keeps the cache warm
considerations.note: Interest matches are served from the account sublist cache, but a cache miss walks the subject tree and briefly takes the sublist write lock to store the result. No-interest results are never cached, so publishing to constantly changing subjects, or to subjects nobody is listening on, re-walks the tree on every message.

considerations.title: Remote interest adds cross-cluster cost
considerations.note: When matching interest exists on another server, the message is also propagated over routes, gateways, or leafnodes, so a publish that looks local can fan out across the cluster. Account for that whenever subscribers live in other clusters or on leaf nodes.

considerations.title: Fast producers can stall on backpressure
considerations.note: If a destination client's outbound buffer fills past roughly 75 percent of its max-pending, the producer stalls until the slow consumer drains. A fast publisher feeding a slow subscriber pays this as latency rather than dropped messages, so watch for it on hot subjects.

steps.label: The client serializes PUB into its outbound buffer in {0} and returns immediately; the server's {1} parses it, matches interest against the account sublist via {2}, and fans the message out to subscribers, with no reply awaited.

operations.summary: Register subject interest (SUB), optionally in a queue group; messages are then pushed.

considerations.title: Subscribe churn steals publish throughput
considerations.note: Registration takes the account sublist write lock, so it blocks every same-account publish match while held and forces all same-account clients to repopulate their L1 caches under an exclusive lock. Frequent subscribe and unsubscribe therefore competes directly with the account's publish throughput — keep subscriptions long-lived rather than creating them per request.

considerations.title: Wildcard subjects cost more to register
considerations.note: A wildcard subscription pays maintenance proportional to cache size: on insert it walks the entire result cache, up to 1024 entries, while holding the write lock that publishers are waiting on. Prefer specific subjects where you can, and be cautious about churning wildcard subscriptions.

considerations.title: Interest fanout scales with leafnodes
considerations.note: Route and gateway propagation is ref-counted, so only the first and last subscriber for a subject send interest updates across those links, though queue-group weight changes always do. Leafnode updates are unconditional and proportional to the number of connected leafnodes, so in a wide leafnode topology every subscribe pays that per-leafnode fanout.

considerations.title: Slow consumers pin server memory
considerations.note: Once registered, the subscription receives every future matching message, and the server buffers pending bytes per connection until the client drains them, so a slow consumer grows server-side memory and can be force-closed. Total subscription count per server also raises match and memory cost — size buffers for the busiest matching subject and prune idle subscriptions.

steps.label: The client sends one SUB frame in {0} registering subject (and optional queue-group) interest; {1} inserts it into the account sublist via {2} under the write lock, bumping genid to invalidate every same-account client's L1 match cache (a wildcard insert additionally walks the ≤1024-entry result cache under that lock), after which ref-counted route/gateway propagation and unconditional per-leafnode updates fan the interest out.

Steady state: no further client-to-server traffic flows — the server pushes every future matching message via a cached sublist match in {0}, then {1} writes it into this client's outbound buffer and runs slow-consumer pending-byte accounting.

operations.summary: Request-reply over core NATS using the connection's muxed inbox subscription.

considerations.title: Reuse the muxed inbox
considerations.note: The default muxed _INBOX subscription is created once per connection and reused for every request. UseOldRequestStyle instead creates and removes a subscription on each request, taking the account sublist write lock both times and stealing throughput from same-account publishing. Prefer the default on any hot request path.

considerations.title: Scales like publish, doubled
considerations.note: To the server a request is just two routed publishes — the request out and the reply back — so its fanout scales with matching subscriber interest exactly as publish does, paid twice. There is no per-request server state on a single server or cluster; responder service time is client-side latency, not server cost.

considerations.title: Cross-gateway replies take the account lock
considerations.note: In a supercluster, service-import replies crossing a gateway take the account lock once per reply and allocate a short-lived reply-mapping entry with a roughly 2s TTL. A single server or cluster pays neither, so heavy cross-gateway request traffic is where request-reply stops being effectively free.

steps.label: The client publishes the request in {0} to <subject> carrying a unique _INBOX.<nuid>.<token> reply subject; the muxed _INBOX.<nuid>.* subscription is created once per connection on first request, and the server matches interest against the account sublist in {2} and delivers the message to the responder in {1} with no per-request server state.

The responder publishes to the _INBOX reply subject; the server routes it back in {1} through the client's single mux subscription as one MSG, which the client demuxes by token in {0} to the waiting request channel; in a supercluster, a reply crossing a gateway allocates a transient reply-mapping entry with a ~2s TTL, tracked in {2}.

operations.summary: Round-trip barrier: flush buffered writes and await a PONG.

considerations.title: The cost is the round trip, not the work
considerations.note: A flush is one wakeup and two protocol frames, so what the caller actually pays is a full round-trip of latency while it blocks, not server work. Flush once after a batch of publishes rather than after every message, and keep it out of tight loops.

considerations.title: RTT reuses the flush round trip
considerations.note: Conn.RTT is measured with the same PING/PONG exchange a flush performs, so sampling round-trip time costs no more than a flush and needs no separate probe.

steps.label: Flush in {0} appends a PING to the outbound buffer through {1}, writes it to the socket, and registers a pong-wait entry before it blocks; the server's {2} parses the PING frame with no interest match or state change.
The server immediately enqueues a PONG via {0}; the client's {1} signals the registered flush channel, unblocking the caller and yielding the round-trip time.

operations.summary: Remove subject interest (UNSUB), immediately or after N more messages.

considerations.title: Unsubscribe pays the write lock too
considerations.note: Removal takes the same account sublist write lock and genid bump as subscribe — blocking same-account publish matches, forcing L1 cache repopulation, and propagating interest retraction to routes, gateways, and leafnodes. Subscribe-then-unsubscribe per request hits that exclusive lock twice, so keep subscriptions long-lived instead of tearing them down and recreating them.

considerations.title: Deferred teardown with AutoUnsubscribe and Drain
considerations.note: AutoUnsubscribe records a max-delivery count on the server-side subscription rather than removing it, so the sublist teardown is deferred until the cap is reached. Drain instead retracts interest server-side first and then processes already-buffered messages locally, letting you shut a consumer down without dropping in-flight messages.

steps.label: The client sends UNSUB <sid> [max] in {0} and returns without awaiting any reply; the server in {1} removes the subscription from the account sublist via {2} under the write lock — bumping genid and evicting matching entries from the L1 result cache (a wildcard removal scans the whole cache) — then retracts interest to routes, gateways, and leafnodes, or, when a max is given, records a delivery cap (sub.max) on the server-side subscription instead of removing it.

operations.summary: Dequeue an already-delivered message from a sync subscription's local buffer.

considerations.title: The cost was paid before NextMsg
considerations.note: NextMsg is a local pop from a channel the server already filled, so the real expense was the earlier publish and delivery, not the dequeue. Budget message cost at the subscribe and publish that populate the pending queue, and treat NextMsg itself as effectively free.

steps.label: Purely client-side: {0} receives the already-delivered message from the subscription's pending channel (s.mch, filled earlier by the connection's reader goroutine) and processes the delivery in {1}, returning immediately if one is buffered or blocking up to the timeout — no bytes cross the wire.

operations.summary: Gracefully drain the connection: unsubscribe everything, flush, wait for handlers, close.

considerations.title: The wait is client-side, not server cost
considerations.note: On the server, Drain is just the per-subscription unsubscribes plus a normal connection close; the time spent waiting for in-flight handlers to finish and buffered publishes to flush is borne entirely by the draining client. Size the drain timeout on the client and do not expect the server to bound it.

considerations.title: Drain time scales with subscriptions and backlog
considerations.note: The client blocks until every subscription's pending backlog has been dispatched and every UNSUB acknowledged, so a connection with many subscriptions or a deep pending queue drains slowly. Prefer Drain over Close for a clean shutdown, but budget the timeout to the expected backlog rather than a fixed value.

steps.label: The client's background drain goroutine in {0} puts every subscription into drain mode and streams one UNSUB <sid> per subscription; the server's {1} removes each sub from the account sublist via {2} under the write lock, so this beat's cost scales with the connection's subscription count (the asset-lock hold).
Once each subscription's buffered backlog and handlers have drained locally, {0} flips the connection to DRAINING_PUBS and sends a PING via {1} to push all still-buffered publishes to the server.
The server's {0} replies PONG to confirm the flush completed; the client then closes the socket, which the server observes in {1} to release any remaining subscription accounting.
On the final close in {1}, when a system account is configured {0} publishes a $SYS.ACCOUNT.<id>.DISCONNECT advisory for the ended session.

operations.summary: Terminate the connection; server tears down all its subscriptions and state.

considerations.title: Drain before closing to avoid data loss
considerations.note: Close does not wait for in-flight inbound messages to be dispatched to your handlers, so anything still queued is dropped and buffered writes that were not flushed are discarded. When losing messages on shutdown is unacceptable, call Drain first and reserve Close for abrupt teardown.

considerations.title: Teardown cost grows with the connection
considerations.note: Cleanup is proportional to how many subscriptions the connection accumulated, so a connection that fanned out into many subscriptions is markedly more expensive to close than a lean one. With a system account configured, each close also publishes a disconnect event that monitoring subscribers consume, so reconnect-heavy fleets pay both costs repeatedly.

steps.label: The client flushes its outbound write buffer and closes the TCP connection in {0} without draining, so unlike Drain it does not wait for in-flight inbound messages to be dispatched; the server's teardown in {1} retracts the connection's interest through {2}, which batch-removes its subscriptions from the account sublist under the write lock (RemoveBatch, O(subscriptions)) and resets the account match cache once.

Concurrently, when events are enabled (a system account is configured), the teardown in {0} spawns a goroutine, and {1} publishes a $SYS.ACCOUNT.<acct>.DISCONNECT billing event recording the closed session and its sent/received byte counts.

operations.summary: Fetch JetStream account limits and usage ($JS.API.INFO).

considerations.title: Scales with account asset count
considerations.note: The usage figures are aggregated over every stream and consumer the account reserves, so a very large account makes each call do more work even though it stays a bounded in-memory scan. Poll it periodically to track quota headroom rather than calling it on a hot path.

considerations.title: A cheap meta-leader read
considerations.note: There is no Raft or disk I/O — the meta leader answers from in-memory usage counters — so a single call is inexpensive. In a cluster every call still funnels to that one leader, so a fleet all polling account info concentrates load on it.

steps.label: The client publishes an empty request to $JS.API.INFO in {0} and awaits the reply; the request is dispatched through the shared $JS.API worker pool, and in clustered mode only the JetStream meta leader answers it in {1}.

The meta leader aggregates the account's limits and usage from in-memory counters in {0} — a bounded scan over the account's stream/consumer reservations — and the handler in {1} returns them as a JSApiAccountInfoResponse; no Raft or disk I/O is involved.

operations.summary: Create a stream: meta-leader Raft proposal, store directories, raft group at R>1.

considerations.title: Provision streams, don't create per request
considerations.note: Creating a stream is a control-plane operation — meta-leader routing, a meta-Raft commit, and directory and Raft state written on every replica. Provision streams at setup or deploy time and reuse them; never create one on the data path or per request.

considerations.title: Cost scales with replicas and subjects
considerations.note: Each added replica means another Raft group to create and maintain, each subject in the config adds to the interest mapping the cluster tracks, and every stream enlarges the meta-state snapshot shipped to new or lagging peers. Size replica count and subject sets to what the workload actually needs.

considerations.title: CreateOrUpdate can cost two requests
considerations.note: CreateOrUpdateStream issues a second update request when the stream already exists, so using it in an idempotent setup path can double the control-plane traffic. If you only need create-once semantics, prefer the plain create and handle the already-exists case.

steps.label: The client sends the stream config in {0} to the cluster's meta leader, where a $JS.API pool goroutine handles it in {1}.
The meta leader in {0} proposes the create to the metagroup Raft, and a quorum of peers must commit it.
On commit each replica materializes the stream through {0} — store directories and Raft group state written to disk.
The meta leader replies to the client with the created stream's info via {0}.

operations.summary: Update stream configuration via meta-leader proposal.

considerations.title: Config-only updates are cheap-ish
considerations.note: When an update touches only retention, limits, or similar fields, it is the same control-plane path as create without initial provisioning: a metacontroller Raft commit and a durable config rewrite per replica. That is bounded work, so ordinary config tweaks are safe to apply on a live stream.

considerations.title: Replica or placement changes move data
considerations.note: Changing the replica count or placement turns a metadata update into a data migration — the server rescales the stream's Raft group and new peers catch up by copying the entire stream. Cost scales with stored data volume, so schedule these during low-traffic windows and change replication factor one step at a time.

considerations.title: Subject remapping re-routes interest
considerations.note: Editing a stream's subjects remaps which published subjects it captures and forces interest to be re-evaluated across the account. Get the subject set right at creation where you can, since remapping a busy stream is more disruptive than changing numeric limits.

steps.label: The client marshals the new StreamConfig and sends the request via {0} on the stream's $JS.API.STREAM.UPDATE subject; a shared $JS.API pool worker on the meta leader validates the change in {1} against the existing assignment (subject-overlap, mirror-immutability, replica and consumer-limit checks) and routes it to the clustered update path in {2}.
The meta leader proposes the updated stream assignment in {0} to the metacontroller Raft group, where a quorum of meta peers must replicate and commit the config-change entry.

On commit each group member applies the entry via {0} and rewrites its live stream's durable config on disk in {1}; a replica or placement change additionally rescales the stream's Raft group and catches up newly added peers by copying the whole stream.
Once the assignment is applied, the stream leader replies in {0} with the updated StreamInfo (new config plus current state).

operations.summary: Delete a stream and all its messages, consumers, and raft state.

considerations.title: Scales with stored data volume
considerations.note: Applying the delete removes every message block and the per-subject index from each replica's file store, so the cost tracks how much data the stream holds. Tearing down a multi-gigabyte stream is real disk I/O on every replica, not a metadata flick.

considerations.title: Cascades to every consumer
considerations.note: Deleting a stream also tears down all of its consumers, so the cost scales with consumer count as well as data volume. Each cascaded consumer is its own teardown on every replica, so a stream fanned out to many consumers is proportionally more expensive to drop.

steps.label: The client sends an empty request in {0} on $JS.API.STREAM.DELETE.<name>, dispatched through the shared $JS.API service pool to {1} on the meta leader, and awaits a single ack.
The meta leader proposes a delete-stream assignment in {0} onto the metagroup Raft log; a quorum of meta peers must commit the removal before it takes effect.

Applying the committed removal in {0}, each replica shuts down the stream's Raft node in {1}, cascades deletes to every one of the stream's consumers, and removes the message blocks and per-subject index from its filestore via {2}.
Once the stream and its consumers are fully torn down, the meta leader returns a success ack to the caller in {0}.

operations.summary: Fetch stream state and configuration ($JS.API.STREAM.INFO); also how a Stream handle is bound.

considerations.title: Plain info is cheap; details are not
considerations.note: A plain STREAM.INFO is an O(1) FastState read answered from memory, but requesting SubjectsFilter or DeletedDetails walks the per-subject index and deleted maps under the store read lock, taking time proportional to the data and stalling ingest on that stream. Ask for subject or deleted detail only when you need it, and expect wide streams to page results at JSMaxSubjectDetails.

considerations.title: Bind the handle once
considerations.note: jetstream.Stream() performs exactly one INFO to validate and bind the handle, so cache and reuse the returned handle instead of re-binding per operation. Re-fetching stream info in a hot path adds a round trip through the shared JS API pool for state that rarely changes.

steps.label: The client sends an INFO request on $JS.API.STREAM.INFO.<stream> via {1} (optionally carrying SubjectsFilter/DeletedDetails/Offset); it lands on the shared $JS.API queue-worker pool where {2} handles it on the stream leader, and {0} issues exactly one such INFO to validate and bind a stream handle.

{0} answers from in-memory stream state on the leader: a plain request returns an O(1) FastState snapshot, but SubjectsFilter or DeletedDetails make {1} walk the per-subject index and deleted maps under the store read lock for data-proportional time, tallying subjects through {2} and paging details at JSMaxSubjectDetails.

operations.summary: Enumerate streams (full info or names), paged through the JS API.

invocation.messages: 1 request per page (1024 names / 256 infos per page)

considerations.title: Prefer NAMES over LIST
considerations.note: StreamNames answers from the meta leader's in-memory assignment map, but ListStreams additionally scatter-gathers live StreamInfo from every stream leader — far more work and network fan-out. Ask for names when you only need to know which streams exist, use StreamNameBySubject to resolve a single subject, and reach for full info only when you need per-stream state.

considerations.title: Scales with the account's stream count
considerations.note: Both variants full-scan the account's stream set and page it (256 infos or 1024 names per page), so the scan cost and the number of requests both grow with the total stream count. An account with thousands of streams turns a listing into many round trips.

steps.label: The client's {0} issues a paged request to $JS.API.STREAM.LIST, delivered to the meta leader and dispatched off its shared $JS.API worker pool into {1}; for LIST the leader full-scans the account's stream assignments and scatter-gathers current StreamInfo from every stream leader via {2}, whereas StreamNames answers directly from the in-memory assignment map.

The meta leader in {0} returns one page (up to 256 StreamInfos for LIST, 1024 names for NAMES) carrying Total/Offset/Limit, and the client's {1} re-requests with a bumped offset until every page is drained.

operations.summary: Remove messages from a stream, optionally filtered by subject / sequence / keep-count.

considerations.title: Filtered purges cost the most
considerations.note: An unfiltered purge is a fast whole-block truncation, but a subject, sequence, or keep-filtered purge walks the per-subject index and does work proportional to the messages it matches. Prefer whole-stream purges where the semantics allow, and expect filtered purges on wide streams to run long.

considerations.title: Schedule purges off the hot path
considerations.note: A purge holds the stream write lock for its entire duration, so a large or filtered purge issued during peak traffic becomes a latency spike for every publisher and consumer on that stream. Trigger purges from maintenance windows or size-based retention rather than synchronously in a request path.

steps.label: The client sends a JSApiStreamPurgeRequest (optional subject / sequence / keep filter) to $JS.API.STREAM.PURGE.<stream> in {0}; {1} dispatches it off the shared $JS.API worker pool to the stream leader, which rejects it if the stream is sealed or DenyPurge is set.
At R>1 {0} proposes a streamPurge entry (capturing LastSeq) to the stream's Raft group; the purge is applied only after a quorum commits it.

On apply each replica runs the purge under {1} — the store does fast whole-block truncation (or block compaction for a sequence purge) for an unfiltered purge, while {0} walks the per-subject index for a filtered / keep purge — holding the stream WRITE lock for the ENTIRE operation, including the per-consumer purge loop, blocking every publish, ack, and consumer op on the stream.

The leader returns a JSApiStreamPurgeResponse carrying the purged message count — at R>1 {0} sends it from the Raft apply loop once the purge is committed and durable across the group, and at R=1 {1} replies synchronously after the local purge.

operations.summary: Read a single stored message by sequence or last-per-subject.

considerations.title: Enable AllowDirect to spread reads
considerations.note: With AllowDirect a get is served by any up-to-date replica and bypasses the shared $JS.API worker pool, spreading read load instead of funneling it through the leader. Without it every get queues through the pool on the leader. Last-per-subject lookups are index-assisted via the per-subject state, so they stay cheap either way.

considerations.title: Cold reads fault a whole block
considerations.note: A get that misses the block cache faults the message's entire storage block (up to several MB) into memory, and the load holds the stream and store read locks, so cold or batched direct gets briefly stall the publish write lock. The cache expires after about ten seconds idle, so sporadic gets pay the fault repeatedly and larger messages cost more per read.

steps.label: The client requests a stored message by sequence in {0}, or by last-per-subject in {1}.
With AllowDirect the request is served as a DIRECT.GET by any up-to-date replica in {1}, bypassing the $JS.API pool; without it the leader answers STREAM.MSG.GET through the pool in {0}.
The store loads the message in {0} to satisfy the read; a cold block faults its whole 1–8 MB extent into cache (~10s TTL).
{0} returns the message to the client.

operations.summary: Erase one message from a stream; the secure variant overwrites its bytes.

considerations.title: Interior deletes are bookkeeping-heavy
considerations.note: Deleting a message in the middle of a block is far more expensive than head or tail expiry: it writes a tombstone and grows tombstone accounting the file store must reconcile on later compaction. Prefer age or size limits to age messages out; reserve explicit DeleteMsg for the odd record you must remove out of order.

considerations.title: SecureDeleteMsg rewrites whole blocks
considerations.note: The secure variant overwrites the record's bytes with random data across the whole block, all under the store write lock, so it costs in proportion to block size and holds the lock long enough to stall concurrent publishers. Reserve it for compliance erasure, not routine deletes.

steps.label: The client sends a delete request in {0} on $JS.API.STREAM.MSG.DELETE.<stream> carrying the target sequence (NoErase for DeleteMsg, erase for SecureDeleteMsg); a shared $JS.API pool worker routes it to {1} on the stream leader.
At R>1 the leader proposes a deleteMsgOp entry to the stream's Raft group in {0}; followers replicate it and a quorum must commit before the delete is applied.

Once committed, each replica applies the entry in {0}: {1} holds the file store's write lock across the target block load and writes a tombstone for interior deletes in {2}, while SecureDeleteMsg rewrites the block's record with random bytes under that same lock.
After the delete is durably applied in {0}, the leader returns the success ack; interior deletes leave a tombstone the file store reconciles on later compaction.

operations.summary: Create (or idempotently re-create) a consumer via meta-leader proposal.

considerations.title: Ephemeral consumers skip consensus
considerations.note: A durable or replicated consumer routes through the meta leader and inherits the stream's replication, while an ephemeral direct consumer on a limits stream is created in memory by the stream leader with no meta proposal and reaped after its inactive threshold. For short-lived or throwaway reads, ephemeral is far cheaper.

considerations.title: Don't churn consumers
considerations.note: Registration briefly holds the stream write lock and, when durable, hits the same meta leader every stream create uses, so rapid create and delete cycles steal ingest throughput and load the control plane. Create consumers once and reuse them rather than per request or per batch.

considerations.title: Replicas and start position add cost
considerations.note: Cost grows with replica count (a Raft group per replica), with the number of filter subjects mapped, and with starting position — DeliverAll on a deep stream sets up a long tail the new consumer must walk. Pick the narrowest filters and the latest acceptable start to keep creation and first delivery cheap.

steps.label: The client publishes the ConsumerConfig to $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE.<stream>[.<consumer>] in {0}; the request lands on a shared $JS.API worker-pool goroutine at the meta leader in {1}, where durable/replicated consumers route (unlike ephemeral direct consumers, which the stream leader creates in memory with no meta proposal).
The meta leader builds a consumerAssignment in {0} and proposes it to the JetStream meta Raft group; a quorum of meta peers must commit it through {1} before the consumer is materialized on any node.

On commit each replica allocates the delivery machine in {0} (pending/redelivery tracking, ack floor, and its own Raft group when replicated) and writes the consumer meta/state file to disk in {1}, briefly holding the stream write lock.
Once the assignment is committed and the consumer exists, {0} returns its ConsumerInfo (config plus delivery/ack state) to the client.

operations.summary: Update an existing consumer's configuration.

considerations.title: As expensive as creating one
considerations.note: Updating a consumer takes the same control-plane path as creating one — a metacontroller Raft proposal, quorum commit, and a durable meta-file rewrite on every replica — with no shortcut for touching a single field. Treat config changes as heavyweight and avoid clients that re-assert consumer config on every reconnect.

considerations.title: Update will not create
considerations.note: The call fails when the consumer does not exist and only a subset of fields are mutable, so it cannot stand in for create-or-update. Decide up front which settings you can change live versus those that force deleting and recreating the consumer, and handle the missing-consumer case explicitly.

considerations.title: Filter-subject changes cost more
considerations.note: Changing the filter subject makes the server re-evaluate interest across the stream, which is heavier than adjusting numeric or timing fields. Batch such changes and avoid remapping filters on a hot consumer.

steps.label: The client publishes the update request (action=update) via {0} to $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE, one of the shared $JS.API worker-pool subjects; the meta leader unmarshals the ConsumerConfig in {1} and routes it to the clustered consumer path in {2}.

The meta leader confirms in {0} that the consumer already exists and that only updatable fields changed, then proposes the updated consumer assignment to the JetStream metacontroller Raft group, where a quorum must commit the entry.

Each replica applies the committed assignment in {0}, updates the live consumer's config in place via {1} (re-evaluating interest when the filter subject changed), and rewrites the durable consumer meta file to disk in {2}.
Once the assignment commits and applies, {0} returns the updated ConsumerInfo on the reply subject.

operations.summary: Create an ordered consumer handle: ephemeral, ack-none, R1, recreated on any gap.

considerations.title: Gaps recreate the consumer
considerations.note: Any detected sequence gap or missed heartbeat tears the consumer down and recreates it — one async CONSUMER.DELETE plus a fresh CONSUMER.CREATE. On a flaky network this quiet recreation becomes a steady stream of CONSUMER.CREATE churn against the API pool, so an unstable link costs far more than the initial handle.

considerations.title: Direct create only on limits streams
considerations.note: On limits-based streams the ordered consumer is created directly on the stream leader with no meta-Raft proposal, which keeps setup cheap. On workqueue, interest, or sourced streams the create still routes through the meta leader, so the same call pays a consensus round there.

considerations.title: Delivery cost is counted elsewhere
considerations.note: Creating the handle is cheap; the recurring expense of pulling and acking messages is accounted under Consume and Fetch, not here. Size those operations for your throughput and treat this create as one-time setup that only repeats when a gap resets it.

steps.label: The client's {0} eagerly issues CreateOrUpdateConsumer through {1}, publishing a $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE request dispatched through the shared $JS.API handler pool to {2}, for an ephemeral pull consumer that is ack-none, R1, memory-storage, with a 5m inactive threshold.

The stream leader's create handler in {1} instantiates the ephemeral, R1 (no consumer Raft group) memory-storage consumer via {0} and replies with the consumer info, which the client wraps as its current pullConsumer; any later sequence gap or heartbeat miss triggers {2}, an async CONSUMER.DELETE plus a fresh CONSUMER.CREATE.

operations.summary: Fetch consumer state ($JS.API.CONSUMER.INFO); also how a Consumer handle is bound.

considerations.title: Monitoring fleets are the real cost
considerations.note: A single info call is cheap, but cost scales with poll rate times consumer count. Dashboards polling every consumer funnel through one bounded API worker pool and each call contends with that consumer's delivery loop, so widen the poll interval and share results rather than polling per subscriber.

considerations.title: The API queue drops under overload
considerations.note: Info rides a deprioritized queue drained only when the main API queue is idle; past its 10k limit the server discards every pending JS API request and emits an advisory rather than blocking. Bursty polling can thus lose unrelated API calls, so cap concurrency and back off when you see the advisory.

considerations.title: Binding a handle costs one info
considerations.note: Consumer() and PushConsumer() each perform exactly one INFO round trip to hydrate the handle, so repeatedly rebinding multiplies these calls. Bind once and reuse the handle for the life of the consumer instead of rebinding per operation.

steps.label: The client sends a CONSUMER.INFO request on $JS.API.CONSUMER.INFO.<stream>.<consumer> via {1}, answered only by the consumer leader; binding a handle through Consumer()/PushConsumer() in {0} issues exactly this one INFO to hydrate it.

The request rides the deprioritized $JS.API info queue, which {0} drains with the bounded shared API worker pool (16 goroutines served only when the main queue is empty); once that info queue passes its limit (default 10k) the server drains every request pending on it and emits a limit-reached advisory rather than block.

The leader's worker in {0} takes the consumer's full write lock o.mu in {1} — the same mutex the delivery loop holds, so info polls serialize against delivery — computes num-pending (O(pending)) and serves ConsumerInfo entirely from in-memory state, then publishes it to the reply subject.

operations.summary: Delete a consumer and its tracked state.

considerations.title: Interest streams turn delete into cleanup
considerations.note: On a limits-policy stream deleting a consumer is pure control-plane bookkeeping, but on an interest or work-queue stream removing the last interested consumer triggers a cleanup of now-uninteresting messages that is proportional to the data those messages occupy. Deleting consumers on a busy interest stream therefore does real disk work, not just a quick deregister.

considerations.title: Discarding pending state costs more
considerations.note: The delete discards the consumer's pending and redelivery state on every replica, so a consumer carrying a large in-flight backlog costs more to remove than an idle one whose tracked state is small.

steps.label: The client sends an empty request in {0} to $JS.API.CONSUMER.DELETE.<stream>.<consumer>; the message rides the shared $JS.API worker pool to the meta leader, where {1} validates it and dispatches the clustered delete.
The meta leader proposes a delete-consumer assignment to the metagroup Raft in {0}; a quorum must commit before the consumer is removed from cluster state.

On commit each replica stops the consumer in {0}, deregisters it from the stream under a brief write lock, and deletes its consumer store (pending/redelivery state); on interest-policy streams {1} runs a data-proportional cleanup of now-uninteresting messages outside the lock.
After the delete assignment commits across the metagroup quorum and is applied, the consumer leader returns the success ack to the client's reply inbox in {0}.

operations.summary: Pause or resume a consumer's delivery until a deadline.

considerations.title: It's a meta-layer config write
considerations.note: Pausing or resuming is not a runtime toggle — it clones the consumer assignment, commits a new PauseUntil through the JetStream meta Raft group, and rewrites the on-disk consumer meta on every replica. That is a full config-change round trip, so it costs far more than a data-path signal like ack or nak.

considerations.title: Prefer a deadline over toggling
considerations.note: Every pause and resume pays the full meta-Raft config write, so using pause as a frequent flow-control lever is expensive. Set a PauseUntil deadline and let it expire on its own rather than repeatedly pausing and resuming — the consumer resumes automatically when the deadline passes.

steps.label: The client's {0} publishes the pause request to $JS.API.CONSUMER.PAUSE.<stream>.<consumer> carrying the PauseUntil deadline; a $JS.API pool worker on the meta leader (the only node that services consumer config changes) picks it up in {1}.

The meta leader in {0} clones the consumer assignment, stamps PauseUntil onto its config, and Propose()s the updated assignment to the JetStream meta Raft group, where a quorum must commit it through {1}.

As the committed meta entry applies on each replica, {0} rewrites the consumer's on-disk meta file via {1} so the pause deadline is durable across restarts (this is a config change, not a data-path toggle).
Back in {0}, the meta leader returns the pause ack (PauseUntil, Paused, PauseRemaining) via {1} as soon as the proposal is accepted, without blocking on quorum commit.

operations.summary: Unpin the currently pinned client of a priority-group consumer.

considerations.title: Only a transient nudge
considerations.note: The unpin lives entirely in the consumer leader's memory and lasts only until the next fetch re-pins a client, so it is a one-shot rebalancing nudge, not durable configuration. If you need a specific client pinned, control which fetch arrives next rather than repeating the unpin.

considerations.title: Keep unpins occasional
considerations.note: Clearing the pin briefly serializes on the same consumer lock the fetch and delivery path uses, so a single admin call is free but scripting unpins in a loop against a busy priority-group consumer can momentarily stall delivery. Reserve it for deliberate rebalancing.

steps.label: The client sends an UnpinConsumer request in {0} on the consumer's $JS.API.CONSUMER.UNPIN subject; a shared $JS.API pool worker on the consumer leader validates in {1} that the stream and consumer exist, that it is the leader, and that the named priority group is valid.

The leader briefly takes the consumer's delivery lock (o.mu) to clear the pinned client id in {0}, emits a CONSUMER.UNPINNED admin advisory via {1} and signals waiting fetches, then returns the ack from {2} — pure in-memory state, so the next fetch re-pins.

operations.summary: Enumerate a stream's consumers (info or names), paged.

considerations.title: Scales with consumer count
considerations.note: Each call full-scans the stream's consumer set and pages it at 256 ConsumerInfos per request, so a stream with thousands of consumers costs several round trips and a scan proportional to that count. Budget for the consumer count, not a flat lookup.

considerations.title: Don't enumerate in a hot path
considerations.note: Because the cost grows with consumer count and spans multiple requests, listing consumers on every operation is an anti-pattern. Cache the result, refresh on a slow cadence, and ask for names only when you don't need the full ConsumerInfo.

steps.label: {0} publishes a request on $JS.API.CONSUMER.LIST.<stream> (one request per page, carrying an advancing Offset) and awaits a page; the request is dispatched through the shared $JS.API worker pool to the stream leader's handler in {1}, which looks up the stream and gathers its public consumers in a full scan sorted by name via {2}.

The leader in {0} returns one page of up to JSApiListLimit (256) ConsumerInfo records from the sorted set starting at Offset plus the Total count; the client's {1} keeps issuing requests with an advancing Offset until Total consumers have been drained.

operations.summary: Synchronous persisted publish: store append, replication, then PubAck.

considerations.title: Scales with replica count
considerations.note: At more than one replica each publish waits on a Raft proposal and quorum commit before the PubAck, so latency tracks the slowest replica in the quorum. Durability comes from quorum replication plus a batched fsync on SyncInterval (default two minutes), not a per-message fsync, so replica count sets the floor, not disk speed.

considerations.title: Message size, index, and header checks
considerations.note: Cost grows with message size and with per-subject index maintenance on every append, so streams with many distinct subjects pay more per write. A large dedup window adds a Nats-Msg-Id lookup, and Expected-Last headers add sequence and subject checks.

considerations.title: One writer per stream
considerations.note: A stream serializes all appends, interest and workqueue acks, purges, and consumer registrations on a single writer lock, so a high publish rate contends with everything else happening on that stream. Shard hot subjects across separate streams to buy write parallelism.

considerations.title: Interest streams can skip the store
considerations.note: On an interest or workqueue stream, a publish with no interested consumer skips the store entirely while the PubAck is still sent. Retention policy therefore changes the real disk cost of a publish independently of message rate.

steps.label: The client sends the message to the stream leader in {0} and awaits a PubAck; {1} receives it on the leader and enters the stream's message path.
At R>1 {0} proposes the message to the stream's Raft group; followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit.
Concurrently, each replica appends the message to its store and updates the per-subject index in {0} (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m).
{0} returns the PubAck once the write is durable across the quorum.

operations.summary: Pipelined persisted publish: send without waiting; PubAcks return on a shared inbox.

considerations.title: Throughput, not cheaper writes
considerations.note: Pipelining keeps many publishes in flight so throughput climbs, but each message still pays the full synchronous Publish cost: proposal, replication, store append, and index update. Reach for async to saturate a stream, not to make an individual write cheaper.

considerations.title: Size the max-pending window
considerations.note: In-flight publishes are capped by PublishAsyncMaxPending (default 4000); once the window fills, sends stall for 200ms and then fail with ErrTooManyStalledMsgs. Tune the window against the stream's real ingest rate, since too small throttles throughput and too large hides backpressure until failures batch up.

considerations.title: Failures surface asynchronously
considerations.note: Because the client never waits, errors including NoResponders retries arrive later on the future or error handler rather than at the call site. Any path that must confirm durability has to drain and check those futures before treating a batch as persisted.

steps.label: The client publishes the message to the stream leader in {0} and returns a PubAckFuture instead of blocking; the send registers on the context's single shared reply-inbox subscription and is bounded by PublishAsyncMaxPending (default 4000, then a 200ms stall before ErrTooManyStalledMsgs), while {1} matches interest on the leader and enters the stream's message path.
At R>1 {0} proposes the message to the stream's Raft group; followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit.
Concurrently, each replica appends the message to its store and updates the per-subject index in {0} (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m).

{0} publishes the PubAck to the shared inbox once the write is durable across the quorum, and {1} resolves the matching future; per-message server cost is identical to synchronous publish, only the client stopped waiting.

operations.summary: One-shot pull request for up to N messages / bytes from a consumer.

invocation.messages: 1 MSG.NEXT request + up to batch-size deliveries

considerations.title: Scales with batch and message size
considerations.note: Cost grows with batch size and per-message size, since every delivered message is loaded from the store and streamed individually. Larger batches amortize the request round trip but move more data at once and hold more state pending ack, so size the batch to what the consumer can process and ack promptly.

considerations.title: Cold reads fault whole blocks
considerations.note: A delivery that misses the block cache faults an entire storage block (up to several MB) into memory under the store read lock, which stalls concurrent ingest on the same stream. Sequential consumers with warm caches read cheaply; random or lagging consumers that keep faulting cold blocks are the expensive case.

considerations.title: Ack-pending and quorum cap throughput
considerations.note: Delivered messages stay ack-pending until acknowledged, capped by MaxAckPending; slow or missing acks stall further delivery. On a replicated consumer each delivery's state update is Raft-proposed and must reach quorum before the batch is sent, so replication latency is on the delivery path, not just the ack path.

steps.label: The client publishes a MSG.NEXT pull request (batch, max-bytes, expires) via {0} to $JS.API.CONSUMER.MSG.NEXT on the consumer leader, where {1} validates the limits and parks it in the waiting queue (pooled, capped by MaxWaiting -> 409 when full).

In the delivery loop, {0} pulls the next matching sequence from the store via {1}; a cold read faults an entire 1-8MB block into cache under the store read lock, which can stall concurrent stream ingest.

On a replicated consumer the leader proposes the delivered-state update (updateDeliveredOp) to the consumer's Raft group in {0} and parks the message in pendingDeliveries via {1} until that entry commits at quorum: delivered state must reach quorum before the batch is sent.

Once delivery is durable across the quorum, the leader streams the batch message-by-message to the request's reply inbox via {0}; each stays ack-pending (capped by MaxAckPending) until acknowledged or ack-wait lapses.

operations.summary: Continuous pull consumption: refilled batch requests, heartbeats, callback or iterator delivery.

steady_state.client_traffic: MSG.NEXT refill when pending drops below half the batch (default 500/250); idle-heartbeat monitoring

considerations.title: Steady-state cost dominates
considerations.note: The expense is not the initial pull but the continuous loop: every delivered message is a store read plus pending and ack-floor bookkeeping, running for as long as the consumer is active. Size the batch and expiry so refills stay infrequent, and note that even an idle Consume keeps paying heartbeat and timer overhead.

considerations.title: Cold reads stall stream ingest
considerations.note: When messages are not in cache, delivery reads from the filestore under its read lock, which momentarily blocks writes on the same stream. Consumers replaying deep history therefore steal throughput from publishers; keep hot consumers current and isolate large replays where you can.

considerations.title: Scale by consumers, not poll rate
considerations.note: Each active consumer runs its own delivery loop, store reads, and timers on the leader, so aggregate cost tracks the number of live consumers far more than any single one's throughput. Prefer a few well-batched consumers over many thin ones sharing a stream.

steps.label: The client sends an initial MSG.NEXT pull request for a batch of messages in {0}, received by the consumer leader in {1}.
On a replicated consumer {0} Raft-proposes each pull so a new leader knows the outstanding request, and delivered and ack-floor state is consensus-tracked.
The consumer leader's delivery loop in {0} sends matching messages to the client as they become available.

Steady state dominates: the client auto-refills MSG.NEXT when pending drops below half the batch (default 500/250) in {0} and monitors idle heartbeats, while the leader's loop in {1} serves each message with a store read plus ack/pending bookkeeping, alongside redelivery timers.

operations.summary: Push consumption: subscribe to the deliver subject; server streams messages with flow control.

invocation.messages: SUB on the deliver subject (+ consumer info validation)

steady_state.client_traffic: flow-control replies and heartbeat liveness responses

considerations.title: Server-paced, but not cheaper
considerations.note: Push delivery has the server drive the pace under flow control, yet the per-message cost matches pull consumption: a store read, pending and ack tracking, and on a replicated consumer a Raft proposal per delivery. Choosing push over pull changes who initiates, not what each delivered message costs.

considerations.title: Idle consumers still cost
considerations.note: Even with no messages flowing, a push consumer keeps exchanging idle heartbeats and flow-control stanzas and arming ack-wait redelivery timers. Many mostly-idle push consumers carry a standing liveness cost on the consumer leader, so consolidate or tear down consumers you are not actively draining.

steps.label: The client sends a $JS.API.CONSUMER.INFO.<stream>.<name> request in {0} to validate the pre-existing consumer and learn its DeliverSubject; {1} answers it off the shared $JS.API queue on the consumer leader.
The leader replies via {1} with the ConsumerInfo that {0} builds — config plus delivery state — and a push consumer is only valid if that config carries a non-empty DeliverSubject.

The client issues a plain core-NATS SUB on the deliver subject in {0} (QueueSubscribe when a DeliverGroup is set), and {1} records that ordinary interest in the account sublist — no further JetStream API call is involved.

The consumer leader drives the pace: {0} loops the stream and reads each eligible message from the store, then {1} pushes it to the deliver subject, throttling with flow-control stanzas so outstanding bytes stay within the pending window.

Steady state: {1} keeps delivering each message with pending/ack tracking (unless ack-none) while {0} exchanges flow-control replies and idle heartbeats for liveness, plus ack-wait redelivery timers — matching pull-consumer cost, including a raft proposal per delivery on replicated consumers.

operations.summary: Positive acknowledgement (fire-and-forget +ACK).

considerations.title: Retention policy multiplies ack cost
considerations.note: On workqueue and interest streams an ack does more than advance the floor: it takes the full stream write lock, scans cross-consumer interest, and deletes the message from the stream store, so acks compete with publishers for stream ingest. Limits-retention streams skip all of that, making their acks dramatically cheaper.

considerations.title: Replica count adds a consensus round
considerations.note: Because a replicated consumer commits every ack through its Raft group, ack cost grows with replica count and is bounded by quorum latency rather than the wire. Where per-ack durability is not required, an R1 consumer skips consensus and coalesces store writes to roughly ten per second.

considerations.title: Out-of-order acks fragment the floor
considerations.note: The ack floor advances contiguously, so acknowledging messages out of order leaves gaps the consumer must track individually until the lower sequences are acked. Acking in delivery order, or collapsing ranges with AckAll, keeps that pending set small.

steps.label: The client publishes a bare +ACK to $JS.ACK.<consumer> in {0} and returns immediately with no PubAck awaited; the consumer leader deletes the message's pending entry and advances the ack floor in {1}.

On a replicated consumer each ack becomes an updateAcksOp proposal in {0}, batched via ProposeMulti in {1} into the consumer's Raft group and committed by a quorum before the floor is applied; at R1 this consensus round is skipped entirely.

The consumer store persists the new ack floor in {0} — at R1 UpdateAcks coalesces writes to ~10 flushes/s per consumer — and on workqueue/interest streams the ack additionally takes the stream write lock in {1} to delete the acked message from the stream store.

operations.summary: Synchronous acknowledgement: +ACK with a reply, awaited until the server confirms.

considerations.title: Synchronous confirmation serializes the consume loop
considerations.note: The caller blocks on the confirmation inbox for every message, so throughput is capped by the ack round-trip latency instead of overlapping with processing. Reserve DoubleAck for handoffs that genuinely must not be lost; use plain Ack, which returns immediately, for ordinary consumption.

considerations.title: Same server work as Ack
considerations.note: DoubleAck does the same Raft-committed ack-floor advance as a normal Ack; the extra cost is the confirmation reply and the waiting, paid synchronously on every message rather than in the background. What you buy is the closed at-least-once window, not cheaper server work.

steps.label: {0} sends the +ACK body to the message's $JS.ACK.<consumer> reply subject as a synchronous Request and blocks on the confirmation inbox; the consumer leader parses the ack in {1}, drops the sequence from its pending map, and advances the ack floor.

At R>1 the consumer leader records the ack in {0} and proposes an updateAcksOp entry to the consumer's Raft group via {1}, stashing the confirmation reply by sequence in {2} until a quorum commits the ack-floor advance.
Each replica applies the committed ack in {0} and persists the updated consumer state (pending map and ack floor) to its consumer store index file via {1}; at R=1 the leader writes it directly.

Once the ack is durable, {0} releases the stashed reply and {1} publishes the empty confirmation to the request's reply inbox, closing the at-least-once window and unblocking the caller's consume loop.

operations.summary: Fire-and-forget negative ack: schedule redelivery, optionally after a delay.

considerations.title: The redelivery is the real cost
considerations.note: The NAK message itself is one cheap fire-and-forget signal that only mutates in-memory scheduling on the consumer leader. What it triggers is a full redelivery, and every retry repeats the entire delivery cost — so the expense lives in how often you NAK, not in the call.

considerations.title: Guard against NAK loops
considerations.note: A handler that chronically NAKs the same messages multiplies steady-state consumer work as those sequences are delivered again and again. Use NakWithDelay to back off, and cap retries with a max-deliver or dead-letter path so a poison message cannot loop forever.

steps.label: The client's {0} publishes a fire-and-forget -NAK to $JS.ACK.<consumer> (optionally carrying a delay) and returns without awaiting a reply; the consumer leader in {1} confirms the delivery is still pending, emits a nak advisory to $JS.EVENT.ADVISORY via {2}, and then either re-adds the sequence to the in-memory redelivery queue and signals a new delivery, or, when a delay is given, back-dates the pending timestamp by that delay to defer the retry.

operations.summary: Fire-and-forget working indicator: reset the message's ack-wait timer.

considerations.title: Replicas turn a poke into quorum
considerations.note: On a single-replica consumer, InProgress is a cheap coalesced store flush capped near ten writes per second. On a replicated consumer each call rides the delivered-state Raft path and becomes a full consensus round, so raising the replica count turns a timer reset into quorum traffic.

considerations.title: Pace it against AckWait
considerations.note: InProgress extends ack-wait during long-running handlers, but every call repeats the persisted delivered-state write. Send it on an interval sized to your AckWait rather than in a tight loop, or the keepalive cost eclipses the work it protects.

steps.label: The client publishes a +WPI body via {0} to the message's ack inbox ($JS.ACK.<consumer>) fire-and-forget; the consumer leader parses the ack in {1} and, if that sequence is still pending, bumps its pending-record timestamp to reset the ack-wait/redelivery timer.

Not just a timer poke: it rides the delivered-state path, so on a replicated (R>1) consumer {0} proposes an updateDeliveredOp entry via {1} to the consumer's Raft group, making every InProgress call its own consensus round that must reach quorum.

The bumped delivered state is persisted to the consumer store via {0} — a coalesced, kick-flushed write (~10/s max per consumer) applied directly at R1, or applied on each replica through {1} after the Raft entry commits at R>1.

operations.summary: Fire-and-forget terminate: settle the message without redelivery, emitting an advisory.

considerations.title: Costs like an ack plus an advisory
considerations.note: Term settles the message and advances consumer state exactly like a positive ack, then adds one MSG_TERMINATED advisory publish. Budget it as an ack plus a small fan-out publish, not as a free discard.

considerations.title: Retention decides the extra work
considerations.note: On workqueue or interest streams a terminated message is also removed from the stream, so the settle triggers stream-side deletion on top of the consumer-state update. On limits-based streams the message stays until normal retention reclaims it, making Term cheaper there.

steps.label: The client fires the ack subject with a +TERM token ($JS.ACK.<consumer>) in {0} and returns immediately; {1} settles the message on the consumer leader like a positive ack to suppress redelivery, then {2} publishes a $JS.EVENT.ADVISORY...MSG_TERMINATED advisory.
On an R>1 consumer, {0} proposes an updateAcksOp entry through {1} to the consumer's Raft group so followers replicate the settle and a quorum commits it.
Each replica records the advanced ack floor to its consumer store via {0} (coalesced flush, ~10 writes/s), and on workqueue/interest streams {1} removes the now-uninterested message from the stream.

operations.summary: Create or update a KV bucket — a stream named KV_<bucket> with per-subject history limits.

considerations.title: A bucket is just a stream
considerations.note: KV has no dedicated server machinery; creating a bucket provisions a JetStream stream with per-subject history limits, direct-get, denied deletes, and rollup enabled, so it pays the full stream-create cost — a meta-Raft proposal, a quorum commit, and per-replica file-store setup.

considerations.title: Scales with replicas and history
considerations.note: Cost grows with replica count, since each added replica is another peer that instantiates the stream and must commit through the meta Raft group, and with history depth (max-msgs-per-subject), which sets how many revisions each key retains and thus the bucket's eventual on-disk footprint.

considerations.title: Create-or-update costs an extra round
considerations.note: CreateKeyValue falls back to an update-and-info round trip when the stream already exists, so an idempotent create against a live bucket costs more than a first-time create; UpdateKeyValue skips initial provisioning when you only need to change config.

steps.label: The client renders the KV config in {0} into a stream named KV_<bucket> (MaxMsgsPerSubject=history, AllowDirect, denied deletes, rollup) and sends $JS.API.STREAM.CREATE through the shared $JS.API request pool to the meta leader, which processes it in {1}.

The meta leader selects replica peers and builds the stream's Raft group in {0}, then proposes an add-stream assignment to the JetStream meta Raft group; a quorum of meta peers must commit the assignment before any node instantiates the bucket.

On applying the committed assignment each assigned replica instantiates the stream and creates its file store in {0}, fsync-ing the stream config to meta.inf in {1}; no message data is written since the bucket starts empty.
Once the assignment commits and the stream leader is elected, {0} returns the StreamInfo response, which the client maps to a KV bucket handle.

operations.summary: Bind a KV bucket handle by validating the backing stream (STREAM.INFO).

considerations.title: Cache the bucket handle
considerations.note: Binding a bucket costs one STREAM.INFO round trip, and the returned handle is reusable — every subsequent Get, Put, or Delete rides it with no further INFO. Bind once at startup and share the handle; re-binding per operation adds a round trip to every hot path.

steps.label: The client's {0} maps the bucket to its backing stream KV_<bucket> and sends a STREAM.INFO request on $JS.API.STREAM.INFO.KV_<bucket>; the server dispatches it through the shared $JS.API worker pool to the stream leader, which reads in-memory stream state in {1} (no disk or raft).

The leader replies from {0} with the stream's config and state; the client's {1} verifies MaxMsgsPerSubject>=1 for a valid KV layout and caches the info into a bound KeyValue handle that subsequent data operations reuse.

operations.summary: Delete a KV bucket (stream delete of KV_<bucket>).

considerations.title: Same cost as deleting a stream
considerations.note: A KV bucket is just a stream, so deleting it is identical to a stream delete: a meta-Raft delete-stream proposal, a quorum commit, and a per-replica teardown. There is no lighter KV-specific path.

considerations.title: Scales with stored volume
considerations.note: The on-disk teardown removes every message block, the per-subject index, and cascaded consumer state on each replica, so a bucket holding many keys or deep history costs proportionally more to delete than a nearly empty one.

steps.label: The client resolves the bucket to stream KV_<bucket> in {0} and sends an empty $JS.API.STREAM.DELETE.KV_<bucket> request through the shared $JS.API worker pool to the meta leader, which handles it in {1}.
The meta leader proposes a delete-stream assignment for KV_<bucket> to the metagroup Raft in {0}; a quorum of meta peers must replicate and commit the removal.

Once committed, every replica applies the removal in {0}, stopping the stream in {1} and deleting all on-disk KV_<bucket> assets in {2} — message blocks, per-subject index, and cascaded consumer state — scaling with stored key/history volume.
After the removal is committed and applied, the meta leader returns the JSApiStreamDeleteResponse success ack to the client from {0}.

operations.summary: Enumerate KV buckets by paging stream names/infos filtered to the KV_ prefix.

considerations.title: Scales with total stream count
considerations.note: The meta leader scans every stream assignment in the account, not just KV buckets, and then filters to the KV_ prefix. Cost grows with the total number of streams you run, so an account with many non-KV streams makes bucket enumeration more expensive even when few buckets exist.

considerations.title: Metadata-only but paged
considerations.note: Enumeration reads in-memory stream assignments under a read lock and touches no disk or Raft, but it returns at most 1024 names per request, so a very large account needs several round trips. It is the same paged scan as listing all streams, just filtered to the KV_ prefix.

steps.label: {0} pages the enumeration through {1}, each $JS.API.STREAM.NAMES request carrying a $KV.*.> subject filter and a growing offset, landing in the shared $JS.API worker pool and routed to the meta leader.

In {0} the meta leader scans every stream assignment in the account under a metadata read lock, keeping those whose subjects {1} matches against $KV.*.>, then sorts and slices the page (capped at JSApiNamesLimit=1024) and replies with the names; the client strips the KV_ prefix to yield bucket names, and no disk or Raft is touched.

operations.summary: Read a key's latest (or a specific) revision via direct get last-per-subject.

considerations.title: Cost tracks value size and cache locality
considerations.note: A hit is an index-assisted last-per-subject lookup, so the dominant work is copying the value out — larger values cost more to read. On a cache miss the server must fault a cold message block in from disk, so buckets whose working set falls out of the block cache pay disk-read latency.

considerations.title: Cold reads stall concurrent writes
considerations.note: The lookup holds the stream and store read locks; when a cold block faults in from disk, that fault briefly stalls the publish write lock on the same stream. A read-heavy bucket with poor cache locality can therefore add tail latency to writes on the same replica.

considerations.title: Reads scale out across replicas
considerations.note: Because a Get is servable by any up-to-date replica rather than the leader alone, read throughput grows with replica count; add replicas to absorb hot read paths and prefer Get over a leader-routed STREAM.MSG.GET.

steps.label: With AllowDirect set (the KV default), the client's {0} skips the stream-leader STREAM.MSG.GET and in {1} issues a DIRECT.GET request keyed by last-for subject $KV.<bucket>.<key> to $JS.API.DIRECT.GET.<stream>.<subject>, awaiting the value on its reply inbox.

The per-stream direct-get queue group (dgetGroup) established by {0} fields the request in {1} on any up-to-date replica, bypassing the stream leader, the Raft group, and the shared $JS.API handler pool, so read load spreads across replicas with no quorum or leader forwarding.

Holding the stream/store read lock in {0}, {1} does an index-assisted (psim) last-per-subject lookup and reads the value from the message block, faulting a cold block in from disk on a cache miss (which briefly stalls the publish write lock).

Through {1}, the serving replica returns the stored value plus Nats-Stream/Nats-Subject/Nats-Sequence headers from {0} directly to the client's reply inbox, or a 404 status header when no message matches the subject.

operations.summary: Write a key: a JetStream publish to $KV.<bucket>.<key>, optionally CAS-guarded.

considerations.title: Scales with value size and replicas
considerations.note: Every write is a full JetStream persisted publish, so per-write cost climbs with the value size and with the replication factor — more replicas mean more bytes fsynced and a wider quorum to satisfy. Treat both as the primary knobs when a bucket is write-heavy.

considerations.title: Conditional writes and deleted keys
