considerations.note: Create and Update carry an expected-last-subject-sequence header, so the compare-and-set runs on the server as a per-subject sequence check with no extra round trip. The exception is Create on an existing-but-deleted key, which can cost up to three round trips: publish, a get to find the tombstone, then a retry at the deleted revision.

considerations.title: History pruning per subject
considerations.note: Beyond the compare-and-set, each write also updates the per-subject index and prunes history past the bucket's configured depth, both proportional to how many revisions a key already holds. Buckets with deep history or many keys pay this on every Put, so keep history depth no larger than you actually read back.

steps.label: KV.Put serializes the value in {0} as a JetStream publish to $KV.<bucket>.<key> and awaits a PubAck; Update/Create go through {1} to attach the Nats-Expected-Last-Subject-Sequence header so the server's {2} runs a compare-and-set on the key's per-subject sequence with no extra round trip.

At R>1 the stream leader validates any expected-last-subject-sequence header in {1}, then {0} proposes the message to the stream's Raft group; a quorum of replicas must commit the entry before it is applied.

Concurrently each replica appends the value to its message store in {0}, updates the per-subject index, and prunes history beyond the bucket's MaxMsgsPer depth (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m).
{0} returns the PubAck carrying the key's new revision (stream sequence) once the write is durable across the quorum; a failed CAS instead yields a wrong-last-sequence error and no write.

operations.summary: Mark a key deleted by publishing a DEL tombstone (history retained).

considerations.title: Delete is a write, not a removal
considerations.note: A KV delete publishes a DEL-tombstone: an empty message carrying a KV-Operation header that is persisted like any other record. It never reclaims space — prior revisions stay in the log within the bucket's history depth, so a delete grows the stream rather than shrinking it.

considerations.title: Scales with the replica quorum
considerations.note: At more than one replica the tombstone is a Raft proposal that must replicate to followers and commit across a quorum before the PubAck returns, so delete latency and cost rise with replication factor exactly as an ordinary KV put does.

steps.label: The client's {0} publishes an empty message stamped with the KV-Operation: DEL header to $KV.<bucket>.<key> via {1} and awaits a PubAck, while the stream leader ingests the tombstone in {2} as an ordinary write, not a removal.
At R>1 the leader proposes the tombstone to the stream's Raft group in {0}; followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit.

Concurrently, each replica appends the tombstone to its store and updates the per-subject index in {0}; prior revisions stay in the log within the bucket's history depth (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m).
{0} returns the PubAck once the tombstone write is durable across the quorum.

operations.summary: Purge a key's history: rollup tombstone that erases prior revisions of the subject.

considerations.title: One write, many deletions
considerations.note: A purge is a single published marker, but the Nats-Rollup: sub header makes the server delete every earlier revision of the key as part of the same store append. Server-side work therefore scales with how deep the key's history was, even though the client sees only one publish and one PubAck.

considerations.title: Replication gates the ack
considerations.note: With more than one replica the leader must propose the purge marker to the stream's Raft group and wait for a quorum to commit before it can ack. Purge latency then tracks replica count and the slowest committing follower, on top of the local store and rollup work.

steps.label: {0} publishes the delete marker to the stream leader on $KV.<bucket>.<key> carrying KV-Operation: PURGE and Nats-Rollup: sub, then blocks awaiting the PubAck as {1} receives it.
At R>1 {0} proposes the purge marker to the stream's Raft group; followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit before it is applied.

Concurrently, each replica appends the marker to its store via {0} and, honoring Nats-Rollup: sub, {1} runs a subject-scoped PurgeEx (Keep=1) that deletes every earlier revision of the key, with the fsync batched on SyncInterval (default 2m).
Once the marker is committed across the quorum and the leader has applied the store append plus subject rollup, {0} returns the PubAck, leaving the purge marker as the key's only current revision.

operations.summary: Read all stored revisions of a key via a temporary ordered consumer.

considerations.title: Scales with history depth
considerations.note: The temporary consumer replays every stored revision of the key, so cost grows with how deep the key's history runs. A bucket caps history at 64 revisions, which bounds the sweep and keeps the consumer short-lived, but a key kept at full depth still costs far more to read than fetching its current value.

considerations.title: Amortize the consumer setup
considerations.note: Each call spins up an ephemeral ordered consumer and tears it down once the replay drains, so even a single-revision key pays that create-and-teardown overhead. Avoid calling History on a hot path; read it once and cache the revisions when you need them repeatedly.

steps.label: {0} opens an ephemeral ordered push consumer via $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE on the KV_<bucket> stream, filtered to the key's $KV.<bucket>.<key> subject with DeliverAll so every stored revision replays, and the create request rides the shared $JS.API pool to {1}.
The consumer's delivery loop in {0} walks the filtered subject, loading each revision from the stream's filestore via {1} — a bounded read capped by the bucket's history depth (≤64).

{0} pushes each revision down the ordered consumer's deliver subject to the client, which in {1} appends entries until pending drains to zero, then stops the watcher and tears down the ephemeral consumer.

operations.summary: Live-watch keys: ordered ephemeral push consumer delivering current values then updates.

considerations.title: Each watcher is a live consumer
considerations.note: A watch is a real server-side ordered push consumer, not a cheap subscription, so N watchers on a bucket mean N delivery machines the leader must feed. Share one watcher across consumers where you can, and tear watchers down when idle rather than leaving them open.

considerations.title: Initial replay scales with keys
considerations.note: On creation the watcher replays the current value of every matching key before going live, so start-up cost grows with how many keys match the filter. Watching a whole bucket replays the entire keyspace; filter to the keys you need, or use UpdatesOnly to skip the replay entirely.

considerations.title: Gaps trigger silent recreation
considerations.note: The ordered consumer is ephemeral, ack-none and flow-controlled, and the client transparently tears it down and recreates it on any detected sequence gap. Recreation restarts delivery and, depending on start mode, can replay again, so a flaky link or a slow consumer that keeps triggering gaps quietly multiplies the cost.

steps.label: {0} sends a $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE request through the shared $JS.API worker pool, where {1} builds an ordered, ephemeral (R1 memory-storage), ack-none, flow-controlled push consumer created 'direct' on the stream leader with no meta proposal, starting deliver-last-per-subject by default, deliver-all under IncludeHistory, or deliver-new under UpdatesOnly, with MetaOnly requesting headers-only delivery.

The server pushes the current value of each matching key as the initial replay (scaled by matching key count) and then live updates to the watcher's deliver subject over the ordered consumer's delivery loop in {0}.

Steady state: {0} reads each new revision from the store and pushes it to the watcher, interleaved with the client's idle heartbeats and flow-control responses; a detected sequence gap makes {1} tear down and transparently recreate the ordered consumer client-side.

operations.summary: List keys by draining a headers-only watcher over the whole bucket.

invocation.messages: consumer create + one headers-only delivery per key + teardown

considerations.title: Scales with key count
considerations.note: There is no key index; listing walks a temporary watcher over the whole bucket and delivers one headers-only message per key, so cost grows linearly with the number of keys. On a large bucket every call is a full sweep.

considerations.title: Cache the key list
considerations.note: Because each call re-scans the entire bucket, avoid listing keys per request or on a hot path. Cache the result and refresh it on a schedule, or track keys yourself as you write them, when you need the set often.

steps.label: {0} creates a temporary headers-only consumer over the whole bucket, through the $JS.API pool.
There is no key index, so {0} replays every key's latest-revision headers from the store — a full-bucket read.
{0} drains the headers stream key-by-key as {1} delivers each header, then tears down the temporary consumer.

operations.summary: Compact the bucket: find delete/purge tombstones via a watcher, then purge each key's subject.

invocation.messages: watcher drain + one STREAM.PURGE per tombstoned key

considerations.title: Two-phase maintenance sweep
considerations.note: PurgeDeletes is a full watch pass over every key's head to find delete and purge tombstones, followed by one STREAM.PURGE proposal per tombstoned key. Cost is the whole-bucket scan plus a propose-commit round trip for each deletion, so it grows with both bucket size and the number of deleted keys.

considerations.title: Serializes on the write lock
considerations.note: Each per-key purge holds the stream write lock for its duration, so a sweep over many deleted keys blocks other writers to the bucket for that whole run. Schedule it as off-peak maintenance rather than during write-heavy periods.

considerations.title: Recent tombstones are kept
considerations.note: Deletes and purges newer than the delete-markers threshold, 30 minutes by default, are retained rather than compacted, so running PurgeDeletes right after deleting keys reclaims nothing. Let tombstones age past the threshold before sweeping.

steps.label: PurgeDeletes first opens a WatchAll in {0}, asking the stream leader to create an ephemeral ordered push consumer with DeliverLastPerSubject through {1} so the client can observe the current head of every key's subject in the KV stream.

{0} pushes each subject's last message back while {1} drains the watcher, keeps only entries whose Operation is KeyValueDelete or KeyValuePurge to build the tombstone list, then stops the watcher so numPending stops updating.

For each tombstoned key {0} issues a filtered $JS.API.STREAM.PURGE (subject $KV.<bucket>.<key>, with Keep=1 when the marker is newer than the 30m delete-markers threshold) through the shared $JS.API worker pool to {1} on the stream leader.
At R>1 {0} proposes each per-key purge op to the stream's Raft group and a quorum must commit it before the purge takes effect.

On commit every replica applies the purge in {0}, calling {1} to drop that key's older revisions from its message store while holding the stream write lock for the operation's duration, serializing it against other writers.
{0} returns a STREAM.PURGE ack for that key and {1} loops to the next tombstone, so the sweep costs one propose-commit-purge round trip per deleted key.

operations.summary: Fetch bucket status from the backing stream's info.

considerations.title: Just STREAM.INFO in disguise
considerations.note: Status is a STREAM.INFO request relabeled with KV semantics, served from the leader's cached in-memory state with no Raft and no disk. The work itself is cheap; treat it as a metadata read, not a data operation.

considerations.title: Avoid polling in hot paths
considerations.note: Because every call shares the bounded JetStream API worker pool and info requests are deprioritized there, calling Status on a request path couples your latency to how busy the server's API is. Read it periodically and cache the result rather than fetching it per operation.

steps.label: {0} calls stream.Info, sending a STREAM.INFO request on $JS.API.STREAM.INFO.<bucket-stream> to the stream leader, where it queues on the shared $JS.API worker pool (bounded at min(GOMAXPROCS,16)) before {1} answers it, with info requests explicitly deprioritized behind other API calls.

The leader's handler {0} builds StreamInfo from the stream's in-memory config and cached state (no Raft, no disk), and {1} re-labels it client-side as KV bucket status: Values()=State.Msgs, History()=MaxMsgsPerSubject, TTL()=MaxAge.

operations.summary: Create or update an object store bucket — a stream named OBJ_<bucket> for chunk and meta subjects.

considerations.title: A stream create under the hood
considerations.note: Creating a bucket provisions a full JetStream stream through the metagroup, so it carries the same control-plane cost as any stream create rather than a lightweight data write; the one backing stream then serves both the chunk and metadata subjects. Do it at setup time, not per object or per request.

considerations.title: Scales with replica count
considerations.note: Each extra replica is another peer that must apply the assignment and persist the new stream's config and filestore, and every replica takes part in the quorum commit, so higher replication multiplies both the create cost and the ongoing footprint. Choose replication for durability needs, not by default.

steps.label: {0} derives a StreamConfig named OBJ_<bucket> (chunk subject $O.<bucket>.C.> and metadata subject $O.<bucket>.M.>) and sends it on $JS.API.STREAM.CREATE, where a shared $JS.API worker on the meta leader validates the config in {1}.
The meta leader proposes the OBJ_<bucket> stream assignment to the metagroup Raft in {0}; a quorum of the metagroup must commit the assignment before it takes effect.
Each assigned peer applies the committed assignment in {0}, creates the OBJ_<bucket> stream and its filestore via {1}, and persists the stream config and state to disk.
Once the new stream's Raft group elects a leader in {0}, the server returns the StreamInfo (JSApiStreamCreateResponse) to the client.

operations.summary: Bind an object store handle by validating the backing stream.

considerations.title: Cache the bucket handle
considerations.note: Binding a bucket costs one STREAM.INFO round trip that only validates the backing OBJ_ stream exists. Resolve the handle once at startup and reuse it; re-binding before every Get or Put doubles your round trips for no benefit.

considerations.title: Cost is independent of bucket size
considerations.note: Binding reads only stream metadata, so the price is a single round trip regardless of how many objects the bucket holds or how large they are. Unlike Get or List, it never scales with the data in the bucket.

steps.label: {0} resolves the bucket's backing stream name OBJ_<bucket> and, via {1}, sends a single STREAM.INFO request on $JS.API.STREAM.INFO.<stream>, which {2} answers from the shared $JS.API worker pool with no raft or disk work.
The server replies in {0} with the stream's StreamInfo, which {1} uses to bind the object store handle; a missing stream is mapped to ErrBucketNotFound.

operations.summary: Delete an object store bucket (stream delete).

considerations.title: Really a stream delete
considerations.note: Deleting an object store bucket is exactly a JetStream stream delete on the backing stream: a metagroup control-plane operation, not a per-object cleanup. There is nothing object-store-specific to tune here — it inherits the stream-delete cost and semantics.

considerations.title: Not a per-job resource
considerations.note: Because each delete is a metagroup round trip that tears down the stream and its data on every replica, creating and deleting buckets per job or per request is an expensive anti-pattern. Provision buckets to live and delete them only when decommissioning.

steps.label: {0} maps the bucket to its backing stream OBJ_<bucket> and sends an empty STREAM.DELETE request on $JS.API.STREAM.DELETE.OBJ_<bucket>, serviced by the meta leader's shared $JS.API handler pool in {1}.
The meta leader proposes a delete-stream assignment via {0} to the JetStream meta Raft group; a quorum must commit the removal before {1} applies the assignment.

As the committed assignment is applied, each replica stops the stream and tears down its Raft node in {0}, then removes the entire message-block store directory from disk via {1}, scaling with the bucket's stored object volume.
Once {0} finishes tearing down the stream cluster-wide, a JSApiStreamDeleteResponse ack is returned to the client from {1}.

operations.summary: Enumerate object store buckets by paging stream names/infos with the OBJ_ prefix.

considerations.title: Scales with total streams, not buckets
considerations.note: The server scans every stream in the account and filters by the $O subject, so the cost tracks the account's total stream count — including KV buckets and plain streams — not just how many object-store buckets exist. A few buckets in a stream-heavy account still pay for the full scan.

considerations.title: Pages add round trips
considerations.note: Results come back in pages of up to 1024 names, so a large account is drained over many sequential request-reply round trips. Enumerate buckets rarely and cache the list rather than calling it on any hot path.

steps.label: {0} publishes a STREAM.NAMES request to $JS.API.STREAM.NAMES carrying a $O.*.C.> subject filter and a page Offset via {1}, queued through the account's shared $JS.API worker pool.

In {0} the handler scans every stream in the account, uses {1} to retain those whose subjects collide with the $O.*.C.> filter, sorts and slices the page from Offset up to JSApiNamesLimit (1024), and replies; the client keeps only OBJ_-prefixed names and re-requests until Total is drained.

operations.summary: Store an object: N chunked publishes plus a rollup meta publish; replaces any prior version.

invocation.messages: 1 meta lookup + ceil(size / 128KiB) pipelined async chunk publishes + 1 rollup meta publish (+ purge of the old version's chunks on overwrite)

considerations.title: Cost is size times replicas
considerations.note: Each 128 KiB chunk is a full persisted, replicated publish, so a Put costs roughly the object size divided by 128 KiB, multiplied by the replica count — a 1 GiB object at three replicas is on the order of 25000 writes across the cluster. Size stream placement and replica count for the largest objects you expect, not the average.

considerations.title: Digest is client-side CPU
considerations.note: The client folds the entire payload into a rolling SHA-256 as it publishes, so every Put spends CPU proportional to object size on the publishing process. For large objects on a busy client this hashing competes with your application work, independent of any server or network cost.

considerations.title: Overwrites cost extra
considerations.note: Replacing an existing object writes the new version to a fresh chunk subject and then purges the previous version's chunks under the stream write lock, so an overwrite is strictly heavier than a first write and briefly serializes on that lock. Frequent re-Puts of the same key add purge load and can hold the write lock against concurrent writers.

steps.label: {0} reads the source into <=128KiB frames (objDefaultChunkSize), folds each into a rolling SHA-256, and pipelines the N chunks as async PublishMsgAsync publishes to $O.<bucket>.C.<nuid> that the leader ingests in {1}; a fresh nuid means a re-Put lands on a brand-new chunk subject.

After EOF {0} publishes one rollup meta message to $O.<bucket>.M.<name> carrying size, chunk count, and digest, tagged Nats-Rollup:sub so the leader in {1} purges any prior meta on that subject under the stream write lock while it stores the new one.
At R>1 {0} proposes every chunk plus the meta entry into the stream's Raft group, where each entry must reach quorum before it can be acknowledged.

Concurrently each replica appends every chunk and the meta message to its filestore via {0} and updates the per-subject index (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m), so a 1GiB object costs ~8200 persisted writes per replica.

The N+1 PubAcks stream back asynchronously as {1} persists each message; {0} blocks on PublishAsyncComplete and, on an overwrite, then issues a stream Purge of the previous object's old chunk subject.

operations.summary: Retrieve an object: meta lookup, then an ordered consumer streams the chunks.

invocation.messages: meta get + consumer create + ceil(size / chunk) deliveries

considerations.title: Scales with object size
considerations.note: A Get delivers ceil(size / chunk) chunk messages through a temporary ordered push consumer, so both latency and server work grow with the object's byte count, plus a per-Get consumer create on the shared API pool. For hot small values, a KV bucket or an application-side cache avoids re-streaming.

considerations.title: Every Get re-streams and re-verifies
considerations.note: There is no partial or cached read path: each Get spins up a consumer, streams the whole object through a pipe reader, and recomputes the SHA-256 client-side, so fetching the same object repeatedly repeats all of that work. Cache retrieved objects in the application rather than calling Get in a loop.

considerations.title: Links add a lookup hop
considerations.note: Reading through a link first resolves the link's own meta and then does a second meta lookup into the target bucket before any chunk streams, so link reads cost an extra round trip. Point hot-path reads at the object directly when you can.

steps.label: {0} first grabs the object's metadata message ($O.<bucket>.M.<name>) with a direct get ($JS.DIRECT.GET.<stream>), which the stream leader answers in {1} straight from its store with no consumer, returning the NUID, size, chunk subject and expected SHA-256.
Client's {0} then creates an ephemeral ordered push consumer bound to the chunk subject ($O.<bucket>.C.<nuid>) via $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE, a request serviced by the shared $JS.API worker pool.
The consumer's delivery loop in {0} loads each chunk sequentially from the file store via {1} — cache-friendly sequential reads with no Raft consensus on the read path.

Each chunk is pushed in order by {0} to the client's deliver subject; the client streams them through a net.Pipe reader in {1} and verifies the running SHA-256, unsubscribing once a delivery's NumPending reaches zero.

operations.summary: Read an object's metadata (last message on its meta subject).

considerations.title: Reads metadata, not the object
considerations.note: GetInfo fetches only the rollup meta entry — size, chunk count, digest — never the object bytes, so its cost is flat regardless of how large the object is. Use it to check existence or size before deciding whether to pull the full object.

considerations.title: Scales reads across replicas
considerations.note: Direct-get lets any up-to-date replica answer the request off a queue group, so metadata reads never contend for the leader or the shared API workers. In a multi-replica bucket that read load spreads across the followers, keeping GetInfo cheap under concurrent access.

steps.label: {0} reads the object's metadata by requesting the last message on its meta subject $O.<bucket>.M.<name-encoded>, issued as the last-per-subject get in {1}.
OBJ buckets set AllowDirect, so the request rides DIRECT.GET.<stream>.<meta-subject> and {0} serves it from any up-to-date replica off a queue group, bypassing the $JS.API pool and Raft.
Through {0}, the store resolves the last sequence for the meta subject via the per-subject index and loads that single message; a cold block faults its whole 1-8 MB extent into cache.
{0} returns the single meta message and {1} unmarshals it into ObjectInfo.

operations.summary: Rename or re-describe an object: publish new meta, purge the old meta subject.

considerations.title: Cost is fixed, not size-dependent
considerations.note: UpdateMeta rewrites only the object's metadata entry, so it costs the same two small control writes whether the object is a kilobyte or a terabyte. Its high cost comes from durably committing those writes through consensus, not from any data volume.

considerations.title: Rename doubles the writes
considerations.note: Updating a description is a single meta write, but a rename adds a second Raft-committed purge of the old-name subject, roughly doubling the cost. Keep renames off hot paths and re-describe in place when the name does not actually need to change.

considerations.title: Rename fails on name collision
considerations.note: Renaming to a name that already exists is rejected rather than silently overwriting the existing object, so treat the collision as an expected error your code must handle.

steps.label: The client first writes the new metadata in {0}, a JetStream publish of the rollup meta message to $O.<bucket>.M.<name> carrying a Rollup:sub header, which the stream leader ingests in {1} before returning the PubAck folded here.
At R>1 the stream leader proposes the meta write in {0} to the stream's Raft group, where followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit before it is applied.

Concurrently each replica runs the rollup-aware write in {0} and appends the record via {1}, where the Rollup:sub collapses the meta subject to just this one message and fsync is batched on SyncInterval (default 2m).

On a rename the client then issues a $JS.API.STREAM.PURGE via {0}, which the server's shared JS API worker pool dispatches to {1} targeting the old-name meta subject $O.<bucket>.M.<old>, leaving the object's chunks untouched.
The leader runs the subject-scoped purge in {0}, applying it to the file store via {1} as a second Raft-committed, durably-stored control write before returning the purge ack.

operations.summary: Delete an object: purge its chunks, keep a deleted-marker meta entry.

invocation.messages: meta lookup + rollup meta publish (deleted=true) + chunk-subject purge

considerations.title: Large deletes stall the stream
considerations.note: The chunk purge holds the stream write lock for time proportional to the object's chunk count, so deleting a large object blocks every other writer on that bucket until it finishes. Delete big objects off-peak and keep chunk sizes sane so no single delete monopolizes the lock.

considerations.title: Storage frees, tombstones linger
considerations.note: Delete reclaims the object's chunk bytes immediately but leaves a small deleted-marker meta entry so watchers and show-deleted listings still see the name; heavy delete churn keeps metadata growing even as data storage is freed. Recreate or compact the bucket if stale tombstones pile up.

steps.label: {0} fetches the object meta (GetInfo show-deleted), then publishes a rollup delete-marker to $O.<bucket>.M.<name> via {1} carrying Nats-Rollup:sub; the stream leader durably writes this single tombstone (deleted=true, size/chunks/digest zeroed) in {2} in place of every prior revision on that meta subject.

Client then issues $JS.API.STREAM.PURGE from {0} filtered to the object's chunk subject $O.<bucket>.C.<nuid>; the request rides the shared $JS.API worker pool and only the stream leader answers it in {1}.
At R>1 the leader proposes a purgeStreamOp entry (carrying the chunk-subject filter) to the stream's Raft group in {0}, which a quorum must commit before it applies.
On commit each replica's {0} runs store.PurgeEx in {1} to delete every chunk under $O.<bucket>.C.<nuid> while holding the stream write lock, so the stall scales with the object's chunk count.

Once the purge applies in {0}, the leader returns the JSApiStreamPurgeResponse with the purged count; chunk storage is reclaimed immediately while the tombstoned meta entry survives for watchers and show-deleted listings.

operations.summary: Create a link to an object or bucket: a meta-only publish, no data copied.

considerations.title: Fixed cost, not data-proportional
considerations.note: A link is one small persisted meta record written through the full replicated publish path, so its cost is fixed and independent of the linked object's size — it never grows with data volume.

considerations.title: Prefer links over copies
considerations.note: To expose the same object under another name or bucket, add a link instead of re-uploading the bytes; you pay a single small persisted write rather than the whole chunked-upload path.

steps.label: {0} marshals the link's ObjectInfo to JSON and publishes it via {1} to $O.<bucket>.M.<name> (base64-encoded name) carrying the Nats-Rollup: sub header, then awaits a PubAck from the stream leader that ingests the record in {2}; no object bytes are copied, only the small meta record.
At R>1 the leader proposes the meta message to the stream's Raft group in {0}; followers replicate the entry and a quorum must commit before it is applied.

Concurrently, on apply each replica appends the new link record to its store via {1} and then runs the subject-scoped Nats-Rollup: sub purge (keep=1) in {0}, removing any earlier meta on $O.<bucket>.M.<name> so only this latest link record remains (fsync batched on SyncInterval, default 2m).
Once the write is durable across the quorum the leader returns the PubAck with the assigned stream sequence in {0}, and AddLink hands back the ObjectInfo describing the link.

operations.summary: Seal the bucket read-only via a stream config update.

considerations.title: Sealing is irreversible
considerations.note: A sealed bucket can never be unsealed, reopened for writes, or have entries deleted or purged. Make sealing a deliberate, one-time decision at the end of a bucket's life, not a step you can walk back if requirements change.

considerations.title: Cost is independent of bucket size
considerations.note: Sealing rewrites only the stream's configuration, never the stored objects, so it costs the same whether the bucket holds one object or millions. The expense is a single meta-layer config commit, cheap in bytes but a full cluster-coordinated change.

steps.label: Seal in {0} first calls the stream's Info in {1}, sending $JS.API.STREAM.INFO to {2} to read the bucket's current stream config as the base it will edit.
It flips Sealed=true on that config and resubmits the whole config through {0} as $JS.API.STREAM.UPDATE, which the shared $JS.API worker pool dispatches to {1} on the meta leader.
The meta leader validates the sealed edit in {0} and proposes an updateStreamAssignment to the JetStream meta Raft group, where a quorum of meta peers must replicate and commit it.

Each peer applies the committed assignment in {0} and persists the sealed config to its stream meta file via {1}; sealing also forces MaxAge=0, DiscardNew, and DenyDelete/DenyPurge, and is irreversible.
The meta leader replies from {0} with the updated StreamInfo reflecting sealed=true.

operations.summary: Live-watch object metadata changes via an ordered consumer on the meta subjects.

considerations.title: Skip the replay with UpdatesOnly
considerations.note: By default a watcher first replays the latest metadata entry for every object, so startup cost grows linearly with bucket size and dominates on large buckets. If you only care about changes from now on, pass UpdatesOnly to skip that initial replay entirely.

considerations.title: Each watcher is a live consumer
considerations.note: A watcher is a long-lived ordered push consumer that keeps costing while open: periodic heartbeats, flow-control exchanges, and occasional gap-triggered consumer recreation. Close watchers you no longer need rather than leaving idle ones running, since each carries the same machinery as a KV watcher.

steady: load meta entry + push to watcher

steps.label: Watch in {0} subscribes to a private deliver subject and sends a CONSUMER.CREATE for an ordered ephemeral push consumer bound to the OBJ_<bucket> stream on the meta subjects $O.<bucket>.M.>; riding the shared $JS.API worker pool, {1} creates the consumer directly on the stream leader with no meta-layer Raft proposal.

With DeliverLastPerSubject the leader's delivery loop in {0} replays the latest meta entry for every object (one message per subject, scaling with object count) and {1} pushes each to the watcher's deliver subject, delivering only meta entries and not chunks so per-message volume stays low.

Steady state: once the initial replay drains, the ordered consumer in {0} idles, and the client keeps answering the server's periodic idle heartbeats and flow-control markers via {1}, while each later meta write is loaded from the store and pushed to the watcher.

operations.summary: List objects by draining a meta watcher to the end.

invocation.messages: consumer create + one meta delivery per object + teardown

considerations.title: Scales with object count
considerations.note: List drains a temporary consumer that replays every object's latest meta entry, so it is a full read scan costing on the order of one delivery plus one disk read per object. A bucket with a million objects means a million of each; the price grows without bound as the bucket fills.

considerations.title: Keep it off hot paths
considerations.note: Because the cost grows with bucket size, calling List on a request path turns a large bucket into a latency and load spike. Cache the enumeration or track changes with a long-lived watcher, and refresh on a schedule rather than per request.

considerations.title: Cost tracks count, not size
considerations.note: The sweep reads only meta entries, never object payloads, so listing is unaffected by how large individual objects are. A bucket of many tiny objects is just as expensive to enumerate as the count implies — control object count, not just total bytes, if you list regularly.

steps.label: {0} opens an ephemeral ordered push consumer through {1} via $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE on the OBJ_<bucket> stream, filtered to the meta subject $O.<bucket>.M.> with DeliverLastPerSubject so only each object's latest metadata entry replays; {2} handles the create on the shared $JS.API handler pool.

The ordered consumer's delivery loop in {0} walks the stream and loads each object's latest meta message from the store via {1} — a full read scan whose cost grows with the object count in the bucket.
{0} pushes each meta entry to the client as a consumer delivery (one message per object); {1} drains updates until NumPending hits zero, then stops the watcher and tears the temporary consumer down.

operations.summary: Fetch bucket status from the backing stream's info.

considerations.title: Cheap read, still a round trip
considerations.note: Status is answered from the stream leader's in-memory state with no disk read or Raft consensus, so an individual call is inexpensive. It is still a full network request-reply, so read it on demand rather than polling in a tight loop, and cache the result if you need it repeatedly.

steps.label: Status in {0} fetches the backing OBJ_<bucket> stream's info via $JS.API.STREAM.INFO.<stream>, which {1} queues onto the shared JetStream API pool's deprioritized info queue (jsAPIRoutedInfoReqs) where only the stream leader answers in {2}.

The stream leader answers in {0} from in-memory stream state (mset.stateWithDetail) with no disk read or Raft round-trip, returning a STREAM.INFO that {1} re-labels with object-store semantics like bucket size, backing store, and sealed state.

contention_groups.effect: All non-direct JS API traffic shares one bounded worker pool (min(GOMAXPROCS,16)) with info requests explicitly deprioritized. Slow calls (subject-filtered StreamInfo, big lists) starve the rest, and past a 10k backlog the server drops every pending request and emits JSAdvisoryAPILimitReached. Watcher/ordered-consumer reset storms multiply entries. Direct gets are exempt by design.

One RWMutex serializes a stream's entire mutation pipeline: every append holds it for the store write; workqueue/interest acks take it exclusively ('only 1 at a time to gauge interest', with an O(#consumers) interest scan); purge holds it for the whole operation; consumer create/delete register under it; gets hold the read side, throttling the write lock. Draining a workqueue while publishing to it, or purging while ingesting, does not scale.

Store appends need the write lock; purges and secure erases hold it for data-proportional time; delivery's LoadNextMsg holds the read lock across cold 1–8 MB block faults (blocking appends); subject-filtered or deleted-details StreamInfo holds the read lock for data-proportional walks. Mixing large purges or cold reads with ingest stalls both.

Random point reads force-load and force-expire 1–8 MB blocks that sequential consumers need warm; interleaving them causes repeated cold faults plus per-block mutex serialization. Mixed random-read / sequential-consume workloads on one stream thrash the cache.

Delivery, pull-request handling, every ack variant, redelivery timer scans (O(pending)), info snapshots (O(pending) num-pending computation), and unpin all serialize on one per-consumer mutex. Frequent info polling of an actively delivering consumer directly stalls its delivery; ListConsumers touches every consumer's lock in turn.

On replicated consumers, every delivery, every ack/term/in-progress, and every pull request becomes a proposal on the same consumer raft group (batched via ProposeMulti ≤256 KB), and delivered state must reach quorum before send. Ack storms and delivery throughput share one consensus pipeline; DoubleAck adds synchronous rounds on top.

Every metadata mutation across every account serializes on the server's js.mu write lock and then a single meta-raft proposal stream on the one cluster meta leader. Bulk asset creation, deletion, or churn bottlenecks cluster-wide regardless of which streams are involved.

contention_groups.resource: account sublist write lock + genid cache generation
contention_groups.effect: Subscribe/unsubscribe/teardown hold the account's sublist write lock (wildcards walk the ≤1024-entry result cache under it; connection teardown is O(subscriptions)), blocking every same-account publish match. Each mutation bumps genid, invalidating all clients' L1 caches, so the next publish per client per subject becomes an exclusive-write-lock cache repopulation — churn converts cheap read-lock hits into a thundering herd of write locks.

Every connect/disconnect takes the server-global mutex several times (client registry, event id) and enqueues $SYS events onto a single drain goroutine. Connection churn storms serialize server-wide and back up internal event delivery.

scenarios.title: Core NATS request-reply RPC hot path
scenarios.situation: A stateless service handles RPC over core NATS: it registers one subscription, then in a tight loop fires publishes, issues request-reply calls, and occasionally flushes to force a round-trip barrier.
scenarios.rationale: On the core data path the server does no disk, raft, or persistent-state work, so cost tracks awaited round trips and lock exposure. Fire-and-forget Publish awaits nothing (0 RTT). Request and Flush each pay exactly one RTT. Subscribe is the outlier: the SUB frame is free on the wire but Insert takes the account sublist WRITE lock (asset-lock) and bumps genid, invalidating every client's match cache and creating ephemeral server state — so registration, not messaging, is the expensive core op.

scenarios.title: KV cache read is cheaper than an API-path GetMsg
scenarios.situation: A read path needs the latest value for a key. One service binds a KV bucket and calls kv.Get; another reads the same underlying stream message via jetstream.GetMsg through the JS API.
scenarios.rationale: Intuition says both are 'read one message from a stream' and should cost the same, but they take different server routes. kv.Get resolves through DIRECT.GET, which any up-to-date replica serves and which bypasses the bounded $JS.API worker pool entirely — so it carries no shared-api-pool choke charge (score 3). jetstream.GetMsg's API variant rides that deprioritized pool (shared-api-pool = +1 choke), landing it at 4. Binding the handle (kv.GetBucket) is a one-time INFO round trip and cheapest of the three.

scenarios.title: Polling ConsumerInfo contends with the delivery loop
scenarios.situation: A dashboard polls jetstream.ConsumerInfo on a tight interval to chart pending counts, and separately polls jetstream.StreamInfo for stream depth. Both look like harmless single-RTT 'info' reads.
scenarios.rationale: Naively these are peers: both are one-RTT, no-disk, no-raft info calls, so an operator would rank them equal. They are not. StreamInfo is an O(1) FastState read (score 2). ConsumerInfo's infoWithSnapAndReply acquires the consumer's full write lock o.mu — the SAME mutex the delivery loop runs under — so every poll serializes against message delivery (asset-lock), and it also rides the shared API pool (shared-api-pool). That double choke charge is exactly the historically notorious stall-delivery-by-polling hazard the per-RTT view misses, pushing ConsumerInfo to 4.

scenarios.title: Fire-and-forget publish is minimal, but the consumer dominates lifetime cost
scenarios.situation: A pipeline publishes events with core nats.Publish and a durable pull consumer drains them: it Fetches batches, Acks each message, and runs a long-lived Consume loop.
scenarios.rationale: The producer side looks like where the load is, but core Publish is the cheapest op NATS has (0): one parse, one interest lookup, no awaited ack. The lifetime cost lives on the consumer. Ack is fire-and-forget yet each is a raft consumer-propose at R>1 (score 5). Fetch adds a store read plus a per-request raft proposal (6). Consume is the workhorse: its steady-state term (per-message store read + delivery bookkeeping + per-delivery raft proposal) is doubled in the rubric, driving it to 16 — the highest-scoring op. The counter-intuition is that the trivially cheap publish and the fleet's heaviest op sit in the same data flow.

scenarios.title: Async persisted publish scores below sync despite identical server work
scenarios.situation: An ingest service must persist a high volume of events. The team debates jetstream.PublishAsync (pipelined, futures) versus jetstream.Publish (await each PubAck).
scenarios.rationale: Per-message SERVER cost is identical: both do a store write, a stream-raft proposal, and materialize persistent state. The only difference the rubric captures is the client's awaited round trip — sync Publish pays 1 RTT, async pays 0 because it does not wait — so async scores 7 and sync 8. The tension is that pipelining feels like it should cost 'more' (or at least the same) since it is used to push higher throughput, but on a per-call basis it is cheaper by exactly the round trip it removes. Both remain tier 'high', which correctly signals the durable-write cost is unchanged.

scenarios.title: Provisioning is control-plane heavy despite single round trips
scenarios.situation: A deploy job connects, then provisions infrastructure: it creates a stream and a durable consumer before any traffic flows.
scenarios.rationale: Each provisioning call is a single request-reply — one RTT — so a naive round-trip count would rank them near Connect. But CreateStream and CreateConsumer route through the meta leader as raft meta-proposals (+2), write store state, and materialize persistent server state replicated on every peer (+2), plus the shared-api-pool charge. That is why both land at 9 (high) while the 2-RTT Connect handshake with only ephemeral state is 5. The rubric correctly attributes their cost to consensus and durable state, not wire round trips — these are not per-request operations.

scenarios.title: Purging a large stream blocks the data path
scenarios.situation: An operator runs jetstream.PurgeStream on a busy stream to reclaim space, while producers are still calling jetstream.Publish and an admin also erases individual messages with jetstream.DeleteMsg.
scenarios.rationale: All three are durable, raft-replicated writes, so all are 'high'. The ordering is driven by contention charge. Publish (8) writes and replicates but holds only the single-writer append path (no extra choke). DeleteMsg (9) and PurgeStream (9) each add the full choke charge: DeleteMsg holds the store write lock across a target-block load (shared-api-pool + asset-lock), and PurgeStream holds the stream WRITE lock for the ENTIRE purge including the per-consumer loop — blocking every publish, ack, and consumer op on the stream. The rubric surfaces that a purge is not just its own cost but a stall imposed on all concurrent stream traffic.

scenarios.title: KV watcher fan-out is a full consumer, not a cache read
scenarios.situation: A config-distribution service has thousands of clients that read current values with kv.Get and also open kv.Watch to receive live updates; some listeners are plain core nats.Subscribe consumers.
scenarios.rationale: A Watch feels like 'a subscription with extra steps', but it is materially heavier. kv.Get is a single direct read (3). nats.Subscribe adds ephemeral state and the sublist write lock (5). kv.Watch builds an ordered consumer over the backing stream and then streams every matching update, so it carries ephemeral state plus a doubled steady-state delivery term plus a choke charge, reaching 11 (high). Fanning out watchers is provisioning-plus-delivery per client, not a cheap read — the rubric ranks it accordingly.

scenarios.title: Object store read/write/delete cost gradient
scenarios.situation: A media service uses the object store: it checks object metadata with obj.GetInfo, downloads with obj.Get, uploads with obj.Put, and reclaims space with obj.Delete.
scenarios.rationale: The gradient reflects escalating server work. GetInfo is a single metadata read (3). Get streams chunk data back — a read with a choke charge (6). Put is a multi-RTT durable, raft-replicated, persistent write of chunks plus a meta digest (9). Delete is the most expensive object op (12): it must purge all of an object's chunks (raft + disk write), update metadata, AND hold the asset-lock during the purge — the same reason a filtered stream purge is costly. Naively 'delete just removes a pointer', but in a chunked object store it is the heaviest lifecycle step.

scenarios.title: Choosing an ack strategy per message
scenarios.situation: A worker processes each delivered message and must settle it: negatively ack for retry (jetstream.Nak), extend the deadline mid-work (jetstream.InProgress), positively ack (jetstream.Ack), terminate without redelivery (jetstream.Term), or synchronously confirm (jetstream.DoubleAck).
scenarios.rationale: Nak scores 0: it only mutates in-memory redelivery scheduling on the leader — yet it is the sneaky one, because the redelivery it triggers repeats the full delivery cost (a steady-state amplifier the per-call score deliberately does not attribute to the signal). The state-advancing settles — Ack, InProgress, Term — all persist delivered-state and raft-propose on replicated consumers (5 each). DoubleAck is the same work as Ack plus a synchronous confirmation round trip (+1 RTT = 6), which also serializes the consume loop on server round trips. The ordering shows that the 'safe' synchronous ack is the most expensive per message.

scenarios.title: Fleet monitoring enumeration paths
scenarios.situation: A monitoring agent periodically calls jetstream.AccountInfo for quota usage, jetstream.ListStreams to enumerate streams, and jetstream.ListConsumers per stream.
scenarios.rationale: All ride the shared API pool, so aggregate poll rate is the real fleet risk. AccountInfo aggregates from in-memory counters with only a bounded scan (3). The two List operations do FULL scans (scan=2) and page over variable round trips (rtts variable -> 2), landing at 5. The rubric ranks enumeration above point lookups because a full scan through the bounded, deprioritized $JS.API worker pool is where monitoring fleets historically overwhelm the server — past the 10k queue limit it drops all pending requests.

scenarios.title: Connection setup and teardown
scenarios.situation: A short-lived batch worker connects, does its work, then tears down — either abruptly with nats.Close or gracefully with nats.Drain.
scenarios.rationale: Connect is the heaviest of the lifecycle trio (5): a 2-RTT INFO/CONNECT/PING handshake, ephemeral server state (client struct, subs map, two goroutines), and a steady keepalive term. Close (2) batch-removes interest under the sublist lock (asset-lock) but awaits nothing. Drain (4) does per-subscription unsubscribes under that lock plus a final flush barrier (2 RTT + asset-lock), sitting between them. This validates that connection churn — repeated Connect — is the hidden cost, not teardown.
